clinical_insight: NPD remains one of the most stigmatized and poorly understood personality disorders. The emphasis on the unconscious wound of unlovability aligns with psychodynamic and schema therapy formulations. The family-systems lens helps therapists maintain empathic attunement. However, the paper is a clinical review rather than empirical, and notably lacks discussion of transference-countertransference dynamics.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic and schema therapy practitioners treating narcissistic personality pathology in adult private practice

title: A conceptual and empirical review of acceptance and commitment therapy for hoarding
summary: This review conceptualizes hoarding through ACT's psychological inflexibility model — as a narrowed behavioral repertoire maintained by experiential avoidance, cognitive fusion, and values disconnection. Three initial clinical trials provide preliminary evidence that ACT for hoarding is efficacious, with the intervention tailored to address insight deficits, low motivation, and executive dysfunction.
clinical_insight: Hoarding remains one of the most treatment-resistant presentations, with CBT dropout rates exceeding 50%. ACT's focus on values clarification and committed action offers a fundamentally different entry point that can sidestep the insight-motivation bottleneck. The three trials are small and preliminary; ACT for hoarding cannot yet be considered first-line. For therapists frustrated by CBT for hoarding, ACT provides a coherent alternative.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT practitioners treating hoarding disorder in adult outpatient settings

title: A Pilot Study of Twice-Weekly Group-Based Written Exposure Therapy for Veterans in Residential Substance Use Treatment: Effects on PTSD and Depressive Symptoms
authors: Natalia Van Doren, Fang Hsi Chang, Amanda Nguyễn, Kevin R. McKenna, Derek D. Satre, Shannon Wiltsey Stirman
summary: This pilot integrated 5-session group-based Written Exposure Therapy into a 28-day VA residential SUD program for 48 veterans with comorbid PTSD-SUD. With 92% completing at least 3 sessions and significant pre-post reductions in PTSD (PCL-5) and depressive (PHQ-9) symptoms, the study demonstrates that an abbreviated trauma intervention can be feasibly delivered alongside SUD treatment.
clinical_insight: The comorbidity of PTSD and SUD creates a treatment dilemma: standard trauma therapies require 12-16 sessions, but residential programs typically last 28 days. WET's brevity (5 sessions, no homework, minimal training) makes it one of the few trauma interventions that realistically fits within existing program structures. The 92% retention rate is notably high. The lack of a control group limits causal inference.
relevant_for: Clinicians in residential SUD treatment, dual-diagnosis programs, and trauma-informed veteran care

title: Efficacy of Anonymous Internet-Based Self-Help for Social Anxiety in a Partially Randomized Patient Preference Trial
authors: Stefanie Arnold, Michael Vogt, Dajana Šipka, Johanna Boettcher, Friederike Fenski, Malte Elson, Thomas Berger
summary: In a partially randomized patient preference trial, 452 adults with social anxiety were assigned to an anonymous or standard 8-week unguided internet-based CBT self-help program. Both conditions produced significant reductions with medium-to-large within-group effects maintained at 24-week follow-up. The anonymous version did not differ in efficacy from the identified version.
clinical_insight: Privacy concerns are among the most frequently cited barriers to seeking mental health treatment, particularly for social anxiety where fear of negative evaluation is the core pathology. The finding that anonymity preserves efficacy is practically significant for iCBT platforms. The preference trial design revealed that patient format preference did not differentially affect outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinicians offering or recommending internet-based CBT for social anxiety, and developers of digital mental health interventions

title: Non-suicidal self-injury as a low-effort strategy for avoiding negative affect
authors: Chloe Roske, Regina Musicaro, Peter Franz
summary: In 149 adults (52% with NSSI history), this study examined whether effort discounting underlies NSSI. NSSI frequency was associated with perceiving non-NSSI regulation strategies as more effortful, and effort discounting was linked to greater avoidant coping. However, avoidant strategy use itself was not independently associated with NSSI frequency.
clinical_insight: This study reframes NSSI through behavioral economics: self-injury may be selected because it requires less cognitive effort than alternatives like cognitive restructuring or seeking social support. For DBT practitioners, this maps onto the distress tolerance module — if patients perceive alternative skills as too effortful, skills training alone may be insufficient. The cross-sectional design limits causal inference.
relevant_for: DBT and ACT practitioners working with adults who engage in NSSI, particularly those with comorbid BPD features

title: Understanding the Unwanted: A Mixed-Methods Study on Negative Effects in an Internet-based Intervention for Depression
authors: Friederike Fenski, Manuel Heinrich, Carmen Schaeuffele, Pavle Zagorscak, Christine Knaevelsrud, Johanna Boettcher
summary: In 1610 participants undergoing an internet-based depression intervention, 7% reported at least one negative effect. These were categorized into treatment-related (format, contact, implementation) and patient-related (symptoms, insight) negative effects. Treatment-related negative effects showed significantly less symptom improvement than patient-related ones, despite no adherence differences.
clinical_insight: This study's distinction between treatment-related and patient-related negative effects has direct clinical utility: a patient overwhelmed by program pacing needs a different format, while one experiencing increased emotional awareness may be progressing. The 7% negative effect rate is useful for informed consent when referring to digital interventions.
relevant_for: Clinicians prescribing or referring to internet-based depression treatments, and researchers studying psychotherapy adverse effects

title: Racial/Ethnic Identity Moderates Changes in Skill Use and Therapeutic Alliance but Not Anxiety or Depression in the Unified Protocol
authors: Matthew W. Southward, Alex Urs, Thomas Adams, Shannon Sauer-Zavala
summary: This study examined how racial/ethnic background impacted treatment outcomes, therapeutic alliance, and skill use in the Unified Protocol (UP), a transdiagnostic CBT approach. Among 70 adults with emotional disorders who completed six core UP sessions, minoritized patients showed weaker alliances and less frequent skill use, though outcomes for anxiety and depression were comparable to White patients.
clinical_insight: Therapists using transdiagnostic approaches like the Unified Protocol should proactively attend to racial/ethnic identity as a moderating factor in the therapeutic alliance and skill acquisition, not just as a demographic variable. Even when symptom outcomes appear equivalent across groups, the pathway to change may differ substantially — minority patients may need more alliance-building and skill-use encouragement early in treatment to achieve comparable gains.
relevant_for: CBT therapists using transdiagnostic protocols with racially and ethnically diverse adult populations

title: Working hard to stay safe:  Effects of anxiety sensitivity on repetitive threat-neutralization behavior
authors: Riley Rozniarek, Annette Rostel, Aardron Robinson, Ryan Smith, Martin Paulus, Robin Aupperle, Hannah Berg
summary: Examined repetitive threat-neutralization (RTN) behavior — a pattern common across anxiety and OCD — using a novel Tap-to-Safety paradigm. Adults with anxiety-related disorders (n=40) showed slowed extinction of RTN compared to healthy controls, and anxiety sensitivity predicted more persistent threat-neutralization. The findings suggest RTN is a measurable, modifiable behavioral marker that persists even when the threat is removed.
clinical_insight: Repetitive checking, reassurance-seeking, and neutralizing behaviors that clients present in session likely reflect the same threat-neutralization mechanism captured in this paradigm. The finding that extinction is slowed in anxiety-disordered individuals supports the clinical observation that simply telling clients their fears are unfounded rarely works — clinicians should expect these behaviors to be entrenched and plan exposure-based interventions accordingly, rather than relying on cognitive restructuring alone.
relevant_for: CBT and exposure therapists working with anxiety disorders and OCD, particularly those with prominent checking or neutralizing rituals

title: Mapping Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: A Proof of Concept for Network-Based Case Formulation
authors: Elizabeth Blomfield, Nimrod Hertz-Palmor, Sharif El Leithy, Tim Dalgleish, Anna Bevan, Gary Brown
summary: Applied the network approach to PTSD by building personalized symptom networks from ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data. Network-derived formulations were compared against clinician-generated cognitive formulations following the Ehlers and Clark model. Cognitive variables predicted affective variables over time, supporting the clinical utility of network-based individualized case formulation for PTSD.
clinical_insight: Network-based case formulation offers a data-driven complement to traditional clinical judgment in PTSD treatment planning. By identifying which symptoms are central hubs in an individual patient network, therapists can prioritize intervention targets more precisely — for example, if negative self-beliefs are a central node driving the network, targeting cognitions becomes the priority rather than a one-size-fits-all protocol. This is particularly useful for complex, treatment-resistant PTSD where standard protocols have failed.
relevant_for: Trauma-focused therapists working with complex or treatment-resistant PTSD, especially those interested in personalized treatment planning and EMA-informed care

title: Targeting Mechanisms of Change as a Path to Precision Psychotherapy in Community Settings
summary: Argues for a mechanism-focused approach to improving psychotherapy outcomes for depression in community settings, where response rates remain stagnant despite scientific progress. Proposes integrating neuroscience tools, brain-based mechanisms, and AI methods to identify change mechanisms and develop simpler, scalable interventions that target these mechanisms directly rather than delivering full manualized protocols.
clinical_insight: Community therapists often lack the resources to deliver full manualized protocols with fidelity. This paper supports a pragmatic alternative: identify the active mechanism of change for a given patient (e.g., cognitive reappraisal, behavioral activation, affect regulation) and deliver targeted interventions around that mechanism. This precision approach could improve outcomes without requiring therapists to adopt entirely new treatment models.
relevant_for: Community mental health clinicians treating depression who seek to improve outcomes without overhauling their therapeutic approach

title: Clinician Perspectives on Process-Based Therapy
authors: Clarissa W. Ong, Kate Sheehan, Joseph Ciarrochi, Eric Lee
summary: Surveyed clinicians about their perspectives on Process-Based Therapy (PBT), an approach focused on precisely matching treatment components to individual needs rather than applying protocols based on diagnosis. Clinicians reported moderate familiarity with PBT and identified both enthusiasm for its individualized approach and barriers including training gaps, implementation challenges, and limited available resources for adoption.
clinical_insight: Process-Based Therapy represents a shift from diagnosis-driven treatment selection to mechanism-driven intervention matching. For therapists already practicing eclectically, PBT provides a formal framework that legitimizes what many already do intuitively. However, the identified training and implementation barriers suggest that adoption will depend on accessible continuing education and clear case illustrations rather than theoretical papers alone.
relevant_for: Therapists interested in transdiagnostic, mechanism-based approaches to treatment selection and those seeking frameworks beyond manualized protocols

title: Clinical Utility of the Level of Personality Functioning Scale: A Survey of German Mental Health Professionals
authors: Leon P. Wendt, Eva M. Klein, Cord Benecke, Susanne Singer, Lena Maier, Deborah Engesser, Yannik van Haaren, Johannes Zimmermann
summary: Surveyed 346 German mental health professionals on the clinical utility of the Level of Personality Functioning Scale (LPFS), a clinician-rated global evaluation of personality functioning aligned with the DSM-5 AMPD and ICD-11 dimensional approach to personality disorders. The study examined perceived usefulness, ease of use, and barriers to implementation in routine clinical practice.
clinical_insight: The shift from categorical to dimensional personality disorder assessment (DSM-5 AMPD, ICD-11) is gaining traction internationally. This survey provides practical intelligence on how frontline clinicians experience the LPFS — its strengths in capturing severity gradients, and its limitations in time-pressed settings. Therapists working with personality pathology should consider integrating the LPFS or similar dimensional tools to track functional impairment across self and interpersonal domains, which can guide treatment planning more flexibly than categorical diagnoses.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating personality disorders, particularly those transitioning to DSM-5 Alternative Model or ICD-11 dimensional approaches

title: Introducing the Causal Graph Approach to Psychopathology: An Illustration in Patients with Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
authors: Jolanda Kossakowski, Lucas J B Oudheusden, Richard J. McNally, Bradley Riemann, Lourens Waldorp, Han L. J.  van der Maas
summary: Introduces a causal graph approach to understanding psychopathology, illustrated with OCD patients. Unlike correlational network analysis, causal graphs allow clinicians to hypothesize and test directional relationships between symptoms (e.g., does obsessions cause compulsions, or vice versa?). The paper demonstrates how observational data combined with causal inference methods can reveal the likely causal structure of symptom interactions.
clinical_insight: In OCD treatment, understanding the causal direction between symptoms has direct implications for intervention sequencing. If the analysis suggests that intrusive thoughts causally drive compulsive behavior (rather than the reverse), then targeting the intrusive thoughts through cognitive techniques or exposure becomes the logical priority. This approach helps clinicians move beyond the traditional exposure-and-response-prevention protocol to consider individualized causal formulations, especially for treatment-resistant cases where standard ERP has been insufficient.
relevant_for: OCD specialists and CBT therapists treating treatment-resistant obsessive-compulsive disorder who want to refine case conceptualization beyond standard models

title: Experiences with One-to-One Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga Sessions: Consensual Qualitative Research
authors: Kelsey Madison Dietrich, Nicole Strumbel, Ashley Baumann, Viann N Nguyen-Feng
summary: Examined individual experiences with Trauma Center Trauma-Sensitive Yoga (TCTSY) delivered in one-to-one sessions, an evidence-based adjunct treatment for PTSD. Previous research focused on group TCTSY; this study used consensual qualitative research to explore how individual delivery is experienced. Findings highlight themes of bodily reconnection, safety, and the importance of the therapeutic relationship in somatic trauma work.
clinical_insight: Trauma-sensitive yoga is increasingly recognized as an adjunct to talk therapy for PTSD, particularly for clients who struggle with traditional exposure-based approaches or who present with dissociative symptoms. The finding that one-to-one delivery offers unique benefits — particularly around safety and bodily reconnection — suggests that therapists should consider individual TCTSY referrals for clients who have not benefited from group formats or who have significant attachment-related trauma that makes group settings challenging.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with PTSD, particularly those treating clients with dissociative features, complex trauma, or those who have not responded to traditional exposure therapy alone

title: On What Do Therapists Agree? Assessing Therapist Evaluations of Emotion Regulation Strategy Effectiveness
authors: Matthew W. Southward, Anne C Wilson, Jennifer S. Cheavens
summary: In a survey of 582 therapists, strong consensus emerged on the most effective (problem-solving) and least effective (concealing emotions) emotion regulation strategies. Cognitive, behavioral, and third-wave therapists rated acceptance and distraction as more effective than other modalities, while rating emotional expression and information-gathering lower. Clinical experience was generally unrelated to strategy preferences, suggesting that theoretical orientation, not years in practice, shapes therapists intervention choices.
clinical_insight: The broad agreement across therapists on problem-solving as the top strategy and emotional concealment as the least effective supports a transdiagnostic, process-based approach to psychotherapy. Notably, CBT/ACT/DBT therapists preference for acceptance-based strategies over emotional expression has direct implications for case formulation — clinicians working from these modalities may need to consciously expand their repertoire for clients whose primary coping style involves emotional disclosure. The finding that experience does not shape strategy ratings challenges the assumption that clinical wisdom accumulates linearly with time in practice.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists and clinical trainers interested in process-based psychotherapy integration and emotion regulation across modalities

title: Autistic Psychotherapists’ Practice and Client-Side Experiencing of Therapy: Therapeutic  Orientation Preferences, Therapist-Client Fit Factors, and In-Session Experiences
summary: A mixed-methods study of 27 autistic psychotherapists found that both as practitioners and as therapy clients themselves, they preferred nondirective approaches, encouraged leaning into difficult feelings, valued in-session exploration of the therapeutic relationship, and emphasized present-moment focus. As clients, they wanted their therapists to challenge and confront them; as therapists, they prioritized warmth and unconditional positive regard. These preferences showed both overlaps and notable divergences from neurotypical therapist norms across cultures.
clinical_insight: This study is among the first to center autistic clinicians own perspectives on therapy, revealing that neurodivergent practitioners bring distinct preferences that may better serve neurodivergent clients. The preference for nondirectivity combined with a desire for relational depth and confrontation challenges purely manualized approaches and suggests that autistic clients may thrive in psychodynamic, person-centered, or relationally-oriented ACT settings. A key blind spot in the field is the near-total absence of neurodivergent voices in treatment development — incorporating autistic clinicians perspectives could meaningfully improve therapist-client fit and reduce dropout among neurodivergent populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with neurodivergent adults, particularly those in private practice seeing autistic or ADHD clients

title: Inhibitory Control Deficits in Borderline Personality Disorder: A Meta-Analysis of Stop-Signal and Go/No-Go Tasks
summary: This PRISMA-guided meta-analysis of 37 datasets from 35 articles found that adults with BPD show small-to-moderate motor inhibitory control deficits compared to healthy controls on Stop-Signal and Go/No-Go tasks. Contrary to common clinical assumptions, emotional factors did not moderate inhibitory control performance. Crucially, self-reported impulsivity did not correlate with task-based measures, suggesting subjective and objective impulsivity capture fundamentally different constructs.
clinical_insight: The disconnect between self-reported and task-based impulsivity is a critical finding for BPD assessment — clinicians should not assume that a patients subjective sense of impulsivity maps onto motor inhibition capacity. The lack of emotional moderation challenges the widely held model that BPD impulsivity is primarily affect-driven, which has direct implications for DBTs emotion-regulation-focused approach to impulsive behavior. Clinicians might reconsider whether some impulsive acts in BPD reflect trait-level cognitive control differences rather than solely emotional dysregulation. The methodological heterogeneity flagged in this meta-analysis is itself a concern: current neuropsychological assessment of BPD impulsivity lacks standardization, limiting its clinical utility.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in borderline personality disorder, particularly those using DBT and schema therapy approaches

title: Language Processing Differences in Individuals with Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)
summary: This review synthesizes evidence that OCD is associated with distinct language processing abnormalities including difficulties resolving ambiguity, hyper-literal interpretation, and heightened sensitivity to threatening language. The authors link these deficits to serotonin, dopamine, and glutamate dysregulation and frontostriatal circuit abnormalities. Preliminary findings suggest language deficits may serve as endophenotypes, with potential for informing personalized interventions stratified by symptom dimensions.
clinical_insight: The identification of language processing abnormalities in OCD — particularly hyper-literal interpretation and threat-sensitivity — has direct implications for how clinicians communicate with OCD patients during therapy. Therapists using ERP or CBT should be aware that ambiguous or figurative language in session may be misinterpreted literally, potentially exacerbating doubt and rumination. The frontostriatal circuit link connects language deficits to the same neurobiology underlying compulsive behavior, suggesting that language-focused interventions could complement exposure-based approaches. A notable gap is the absence of symptom-dimension-specific language profiles — contamination-related OCD may involve different language biases than harm-related OCD, which could guide tailored treatment planning.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD, especially those using ERP and CBT, who should consider how communication style may interact with language processing biases

title: Adverse experiences of women with undiagnosed ADHD and the invaluable role of diagnosis
summary: A mixed-methods survey of 28 women with late-diagnosed ADHD revealed pervasive patterns of criticism, lack of support from medical professionals, and internalized shame across the lifespan. Participants described decades of low self-esteem, guilt, and negative self-perception attributed to undiagnosed ADHD. Receiving a diagnosis was experienced as revelatory, with participants reporting improved self-esteem, a sense that their lives finally made sense, and reduced suicidal ideation. Many grieved the lives they could have led with earlier identification.
clinical_insight: This study powerfully illustrates how ADHD in women is routinely misattributed to character flaws, anxiety, or depression, leading to cascading harm across developmental stages. For private practice clinicians, the key takeaway is the critical importance of screening for ADHD in adult women presenting with treatment-resistant depression, anxiety, or chronic low self-esteem — especially when standard treatments have been partially or fully ineffective. The diagnostic revelation narrative reported by participants underscores that assessment itself can be therapeutic. A significant blind spot is the continuing reliance on male-normed ADHD presentations in screening tools, which systematically miss the inattentive subtype predominantly seen in women.
relevant_for: Clinicians in adult private practice, particularly those seeing women with treatment-resistant mood and anxiety presentations

title: Cognitive performance in functional neurological disorder: A systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: L.S. Merritt Millman, Isobel A Williams, Johannes Jungilligens, Susannah Pick
summary: A systematic review of 56 studies with 2260 FND patients found mixed and inconsistent evidence for objective cognitive deficits across executive functioning, attention, memory, and processing speed domains. Only 24% of studies assessed performance validity. Meta-analyses revealed that functional seizure patients actually outperformed epilepsy patients on naming and long-term memory tasks, with no significant differences in working memory or cognitive flexibility. Common confounds included demographic differences, medication effects, and intellectual functioning disparities between groups.
clinical_insight: The finding that FND patients do not consistently show objective cognitive deficits — and may even outperform epilepsy patients on some measures — is crucial for case conceptualization. Clinicians should be cautious about attributing functional symptoms to cognitive impairment without formal neuropsychological testing and performance validity assessment. The remarkably low rate of performance validity testing (24%) is a methodological blind spot that limits the interpretability of the entire literature. For therapists working with FND patients, this supports a model where symptoms are better understood through a biopsychosocial framework rather than a cognitive deficit model, and where treatment should focus on functional recovery and symptom management rather than cognitive rehabilitation.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and therapists working with functional neurological disorder, conversion disorder, and psychosomatic presentations

title: High-threshold-low-tolerance: a model for the emotionality paradox in PTSD
authors: Nachshon Korem, Or Duek, Tobias Raphael Spiller, Ziv Ben-Zion, Ifat Levy, Ilan Harpaz-Rotem
summary: Using hierarchical Bayesian modeling of 1045 trauma-exposed individuals rating valenced images, this study found that individuals with probable PTSD exhibited a steeper emotional transition slope — shifting rapidly between neutral and negative emotional states. Emotional numbing severity significantly predicted this steeper slope, suggesting that numbing and emotional volatility are not opposing symptoms but mechanistically linked. The authors propose a high-threshold-low-tolerance model where emotional numbing narrows the window of emotional regulation, creating abrupt shifts to intense negative affect.
clinical_insight: This computational model directly challenges the common clinical separation of PTSDs emotional numbing and hyperarousal clusters by showing they are mechanistically intertwined. For trauma-focused therapists using EMDR, PE, or CPT, this reframing is significant: addressing numbing in treatment may simultaneously reduce emotional volatility, and vice versa. The model suggests that mindfulness-based emotion regulation strategies, which widen the window between neutral and negative states, could be particularly effective for patients with prominent numbing symptoms. A blind spot in current PTSD treatment protocols is the tendency to address numbing and reactivity as separate targets — this model argues for an integrated approach that targets the transition dynamic itself rather than individual symptom clusters.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists using EMDR, CPT, PE, or body-based approaches working with PTSD and CPTSD presentations involving emotional numbing

title: Chronic loneliness: neurocognitive mechanisms and interventions
authors: Mitjan Morr, Xiqin Liu, Rene Hurlemann, Benjamin Becker, Dirk Scheele
summary: This review examines neurocognitive mechanisms underlying chronic loneliness, including hypervigilance to social threat, negative social attribution biases, and altered neural processing in social cognition networks. The authors integrate findings on brain alterations linked to social disconnectedness with therapeutic intervention approaches, arguing that targeting specific neurocognitive mechanisms — rather than simply increasing social contact — is essential for effective loneliness treatment in both healthy individuals and psychiatric populations.
clinical_insight: Chronic loneliness is frequently comorbid with depression, anxiety, and personality disorders in adult private practice, yet is rarely addressed as a primary treatment target. This review supports a shift from purely social interventions to cognitively-focused approaches that target the underlying hypervigilance and negative social attribution biases maintaining loneliness. CBT-based social cognition interventions, compassion-focused therapy, and schema therapys work on early maladaptive schemas around social exclusion could directly address these mechanisms. The reviews emphasis on neurocognitive specificity is a strength, though a blind spot is the limited discussion of how loneliness intersects with attachment styles and early relational trauma — both highly relevant for the therapy populations most affected by chronic loneliness.
relevant_for: Clinicians in adult private practice whose clients present with chronic loneliness comorbid with depression, social anxiety, or personality pathology

title: Identifying (Effective) Elements in the Psychological Treatment of Suicidality: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
authors: Nikki van Eijk, Daan Creemers, Alexia Marchand, Remco de Winter, Lizanne Schweren, Renske Gilissen, Wouter van Ballegooijen
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis breaks down psychotherapeutic interventions for suicidality into individual components rather than evaluating whole treatment packages. By analyzing which specific elements (e.g., safety planning, cognitive restructuring, distress tolerance skills) are associated with reductions in suicidal ideation and behavior, the study aims to move beyond brand-name therapy comparisons toward identifying the active ingredients that make suicidality interventions effective.
clinical_insight: Component-level analysis of suicidality treatments is a paradigm shift from the dominant model of comparing whole therapy packages. For clinicians, this means that rather than asking which manual to follow, the field is moving toward asking which specific techniques to deploy for which suicidal presentation. The tension here is between protocol fidelity and component flexibility: while manualized approaches like DBT and CBT for suicide prevention have strong evidence bases, this component approach may reveal that certain elements (e.g., means restriction counseling, crisis response planning) are the true drivers of change. A notable blind spot is that most suicidality trials still exclude the most acutely at-risk patients, limiting the generalizability of these findings to private practice settings where high-risk clients are the norm.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with suicidal patients across modalities, particularly those in crisis intervention and DBT-informed practice

title: "I’m Not My True Self": A Systematic Review Exploring the Association Between Camouflaging and Self-Related Factors in Autistic Adults
authors: Celeste Andrade, Nallely Cáceres, Amanda Escobar, Catalina Fuentes, Javier Muñoz, Connor Tom Keating, Juan Carlos Oliveros
summary: This systematic review examines the relationship between camouflaging (masking autistic traits in social contexts) and self-related factors in autistic adults, including self-esteem, self-acceptance, and identity coherence. Findings consistently show that higher camouflaging is associated with lower self-esteem, poorer self-acceptance, and a fragmented sense of identity, with mental health outcomes mediating many of these relationships.
clinical_insight: Camouflaging is increasingly recognized as a key mechanism driving mental health deterioration in autistic adults, yet it rarely appears in standard clinical assessments. For therapists, this review supports routinely assessing camouflaging behaviors in autistic clients, particularly those presenting with depression, anxiety, or identity-related distress. The therapeutic implication is that treatment goals should include reducing camouflaging rather than merely managing its consequences. However, a critical blind spot is that most research conceptualizes camouflaging as an individual deficit rather than a response to an inaccessible neurotypical social world, which risks pathologizing adaptive survival strategies. Clinicians should balance validation of the social pressures driving camouflaging with exploration of its personal costs.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with autistic adults in private practice, particularly those seeing late-diagnosed women and non-binary individuals who present with camouflaging-related burnout

title: Associations between Personality Profiles and Suicide-Related Risk Factors from the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide
authors: Martina Fruhbauerova, Megan L. Rogers, Julie Cerel
summary: This study examines how Five Factor Model personality profiles relate to suicide-related risk factors derived from the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide (ITS), specifically thwarted belongingness, perceived burdensomeness, and hopelessness. By mapping personality configurations onto ITS constructs, the research identifies which personality profiles carry the highest suicide risk and how these dynamics interact.
clinical_insight: Integrating personality profiling with the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide offers clinicians a more nuanced risk assessment framework than either approach alone. The finding that specific personality configurations (particularly high neuroticism combined with low agreeableness) map onto ITS risk factors has direct implications for case formulation: patients with these profiles may need targeted interventions addressing both personality-level patterns (e.g., schema work for maladaptive interpersonal schemas) and acute ITS factors (e.g., belongingness interventions). The tension in the literature between trait-based and state-based suicide risk models remains unresolved; this study bridges them but cannot establish causality. A blind spot is the near-exclusive reliance on self-report measures of personality, which may be distorted in acutely suicidal patients.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments and personality-guided case formulation in adult inpatient and outpatient settings

title: A DOCTORAL THESIS EXAMINING THE FIELD OF THERAPIST DRIFT IN COGNITIVE BEHAVIOURAL THERAPY, AND THE EXPLORATION OF META-COMPETENCE IN COGNITIVE BEHAVIOURAL THERAPY
authors: Denika Campbell-Lee, Dr. Stephen Barton, Mr Peter Armstrong
summary: This doctoral thesis combines a scoping review of therapist drift in CBT with an empirical study comparing meta-competence (the capacity to know why and when specific skills are needed) across trainee, recently-qualified, and experienced CBT therapists. Therapist drift, the gradual departure from evidence-based practice, is examined as a systemic problem rather than an individual failing. The empirical component identifies higher-order CBT skills that distinguish experienced therapists from trainees when managing complex atypical presentations.
clinical_insight: Therapist drift is one of the most under-acknowledged threats to treatment efficacy in routine practice. This thesis provides empirical evidence that meta-competence, the ability to flexibly adapt protocols to atypical presentations while maintaining fidelity to core principles, develops with experience but is not guaranteed by it. For CBT supervisors and training programs, the implication is that supervision should explicitly target meta-competence rather than just protocol adherence. The scoping review findings on drift are particularly relevant for private practitioners who work without regular supervision, where drift risk is highest. A blind spot is the lack of research on drift in third-wave therapies (ACT, DBT, schema therapy) where protocol flexibility is inherent rather than exceptional.
relevant_for: CBT therapists, clinical supervisors, and training program directors concerned with maintaining treatment fidelity and developing therapist meta-competence

title: Identifying higher-order CBT skills: Is there a difference between experienced and trainee therapists regarding meta-competence?
authors: Denika Campbell-Lee, Dr. Stephen Barton, Mr Peter Armstrong
summary: This empirical study identifies and compares higher-order CBT meta-competencies across three therapist groups: trainees, recently qualified, and experienced practitioners. Using therapist judgment tasks involving complex client scenarios where standard protocols do not map neatly onto presentations, the study isolates the skills that distinguish experienced therapists, particularly the capacity for flexible conceptualization and adaptive protocol modification.
clinical_insight: This is a rare empirical investigation into what actually distinguishes effective experienced therapists from competent novices beyond simple years of practice. The finding that meta-competence, specifically the ability to recognize when standard protocols need adaptation and how to accomplish that adaptation without losing fidelity, is a distinguishable skill set has implications for both training and supervision. For private practice clinicians working with complex comorbid presentations that rarely fit textbook protocols, this validates the intuitive clinical judgment that experienced therapists develop. The tension with manualized treatment approaches is clear: the field demands both fidelity and flexibility, yet training typically emphasizes the former while the latter is left to implicit learning. A blind spot is that this study examines CBT specifically, and whether similar meta-competencies exist and differ across other modalities (psychodynamic, schema therapy, IFS) remains unknown.
relevant_for: CBT clinicians and supervisors interested in understanding and developing advanced clinical reasoning beyond protocol adherence

title: Dynamics of narcissistic grandiosity and vulnerability in naturalistic and experimental settings
authors: Elizabeth A. Edershile, Anna Szücs, Alexandre Dombrovski, Aidan G.C. Wright
summary: This study integrates ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and experimental methods to examine how narcissistic grandiosity and vulnerability fluctuate in response to social feedback. Using naturalistic daily-life assessments alongside controlled lab paradigms involving status threats, the research maps the dynamic interplay between these two narcissistic states and tests theoretical accounts of rapid state-shifting in narcissism.
clinical_insight: The dynamic model of narcissism has significant implications for clinical work with personality-disordered clients. Rather than viewing grandiosity and vulnerability as stable traits, this EMA-based approach reveals them as rapidly fluctuating states triggered by interpersonal context. For therapists using schema therapy, psychodynamic approaches, or IFS with narcissistic patients, this supports pacing interventions around the clients current self-state rather than confronting grandiosity when vulnerability is accessible. The experimental finding that status threats catalyze state shifts has direct implications for therapeutic rupture and repair: challenges to the patients self-perception in session may trigger rapid defensive shifts that derail treatment if not anticipated. A blind spot is that most narcissism research still relies on non-clinical samples, and how these dynamics manifest in clinically diagnosed NPD patients, particularly those in private practice seeking help voluntarily, may differ substantially.
relevant_for: Therapists working with narcissistic personality pathology, particularly those using schema therapy, psychodynamic, or IFS approaches

title: Longitudinal interplay between co-occurring alcohol use, mood, and functioning in bipolar spectrum disorders
authors: Sarah Havens Sperry, Audrey Stromberg, Victoria Adams Murphy, Carly A Lasagna, Margo W. Menkes, Melvin G McInnis, Anastasia Yocum, Ivy Fei Tso
summary: This pre-registered longitudinal study tracks 663 individuals with bipolar spectrum disorders over an average of 10 years, characterizing the temporal dynamics between alcohol use, mood episodes, and life functioning. The study identifies patterns of comorbid alcohol use disorder in BD and delineates how drinking, mood symptoms, and functional impairment influence each other over time, revealing bidirectional relationships that challenge unidirectional causal models.
clinical_insight: The 10-year longitudinal design with 663 participants provides unusually robust evidence for the bidirectional relationship between alcohol use and mood instability in bipolar disorder. For clinicians managing comorbid BD and AUD, this study supports integrated treatment approaches that address both conditions simultaneously rather than sequentially. The finding that alcohol use both precipitates and is used to manage mood episodes creates a vicious cycle that standard mood stabilizer pharmacotherapy alone cannot break. Clinicians should routinely assess alcohol use patterns at every session with BD patients and consider integrated CBT approaches that target both mood regulation and substance use coping. A blind spot is the study does not examine whether specific bipolar subtypes (I vs. II) show different alcohol-mood dynamics, which could guide more targeted intervention planning.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating bipolar disorder in private practice or community settings where comorbid alcohol use is common

title: Virtually delivered guided self-help for binge eating disorder and bulimia nervosa: Findings from a service evaluation conducted during the COVID-19 pandemic
authors: Bethan Dalton, Molly R Davies, Chloe Hutchings-Hay, Rachel Potterton, Eleanor Breen O'Byrne, Charmaine Kilonzo, Michaela Flynn, Stefano R. Belli, Lucy Gallop, Gemma Gordon, Johanna Louise Keeler, Imelda Minnock, Matthew Phillips, Lauren Robinson, Emma Snashall, Cindy Toloza, Luiza Walo, Jason Cole, Ulrike Schmidt
summary: This service evaluation examines the effectiveness of videoconferencing-delivered guided self-help (GSH) for adults with bulimia nervosa and binge eating disorder. Assessing uptake, completion rates, and clinical outcomes during the COVID-19 pandemic, the study extends prior research on GSH for bulimic spectrum eating disorders by testing an innovative delivery modality that could expand treatment access while maintaining clinical effectiveness.
clinical_insight: Guided self-help via videoconferencing represents a pragmatic solution to the chronic capacity constraints in eating disorder services. For private practitioners, this model offers a potential framework for scaling clinical impact: providing structured evidence-based guidance (typically CBT-E or CBT-BN based) with fewer sessions than full therapy. The service evaluation design, while less rigorous than an RCT, provides real-world effectiveness data that is directly applicable to clinical practice decisions. A key tension is between the accessibility advantages of virtual GSH and the therapeutic depth that in-person full protocol CBT-E provides. For complex cases with significant comorbidity (personality disorders, trauma histories), GSH alone is likely insufficient. The blind spot is the absence of long-term follow-up data, meaning it remains unclear whether virtual GSH produces durable recovery or simply accelerates initial symptom reduction.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders who are considering guided self-help or telehealth delivery models to expand treatment access

title: A computational lens on the transdiagnostic nature of intolerance of uncertainty
authors: Tim Sandhu, Bowen Xiao, Rebecca P. Lawson
summary: This computational psychiatry review reframes intolerance of uncertainty (IU) as a transdiagnostic mechanism operating across psychiatric and neurodevelopmental disorders. Using computational models from cognitive science, the authors propose that apparent difficulties in coping with uncertainty reflect specific computational processes (e.g., precision weighting, belief updating) that can be quantitatively modeled, offering a unifying framework for understanding IU in GAD, OCD, autism, and other conditions.
clinical_insight: Reframing IU through a computational lens has significant clinical implications beyond academic modeling. For therapists using CBT, ACT, or metacognitive therapy, this framework suggests that IU is not a monolithic trait but a composite of distinct computational processes that may require different intervention targets. For example, a client who struggles with precision weighting (overweighing threatening information under uncertainty) may benefit more from cognitive restructuring of threat appraisals, while a client with impaired belief updating may respond better to behavioral experiments that directly test predictions. This challenges the common clinical practice of applying uniform IU-targeting interventions across diagnoses. The blind spot is that computational models, while theoretically elegant, have not yet been translated into practical clinical tools that therapists can use in session without specialized training.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT clinicians working with GAD, OCD, and health anxiety where intolerance of uncertainty is a central maintaining factor

title: Transdiagnostic associations with interpersonal and affective variability in borderline personality pathology
authors: Whitney R. Ringwald, Michael Hallquist, Alexandre Dombrovski, Aidan G.C. Wright
summary: Using ambulatory assessment, this study maps indices of variability in affect, interpersonal behavior, and perceptions of others onto general and specific dimensions of personality pathology in BPD. Rather than treating BPD as a categorical diagnosis, the study examines how emotional and behavioral variability, core features of BPD, relate to broader personality pathology dimensions, testing whether BPD-specific variability patterns exist or whether they reflect general personality dysfunction.
clinical_insight: This study challenges the categorical framing of BPD by showing that the hallmark variability in affect and interpersonal behavior may reflect general personality pathology rather than BPD-specific processes. For clinicians, this supports a dimensional approach to case formulation: rather than asking does this patient have BPD, the question becomes which dimensions of personality dysfunction are most prominent and variable. This has direct implications for treatment selection: if a patients interpersonal variability reflects general personality pathology rather than BPD-specific dynamics, then transdiagnostic approaches (schema therapy, CFT, ACT) may be as appropriate as BPD-specific treatments like DBT. The tension between BPD-specific and transdiagnostic models has real clinical consequences for treatment planning and insurance reimbursement. A blind spot is that ambulatory assessment captures quantity and pattern of variability but not the subjective meaning of interpersonal episodes for the patient, which is precisely what psychodynamic and schema-focused therapies target.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with personality pathology who are navigating the tension between BPD-specific and transdiagnostic treatment approaches

title: Adverse Childhood Experiences and Adult Psychopathology: A Latent Class Analysis Approach
summary: This study uses latent class analysis to identify distinct patterns of adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) and their differential associations with co-occurring adult psychopathology, rather than relying on simple cumulative ACE scores. Results reveal that specific ACE profiles, such as emotional abuse combined with household dysfunction, predict different psychopathology patterns compared to profiles dominated by physical neglect or household substance use, demonstrating that ACE type matters beyond sheer quantity.
clinical_insight: The shift from cumulative ACE scores to latent class profiling has direct implications for case formulation in trauma-focused therapy. Rather than asking how many ACEs a client experienced, clinicians should map the specific pattern of adversity, as this study shows that different ACE profiles map onto different adult psychopathology clusters. For EMDR and schema therapy practitioners, this supports assessing not just trauma presence but trauma type when developing treatment plans. The tension in the literature between dimensional (cumulative) and categorical (typological) ACE models remains unresolved; this study supports the typological approach but cannot determine whether the identified classes reflect causal mechanisms or correlated risk factors. A blind spot is the reliance on retrospective self-report of ACEs, which is subject to recall bias and may underestimate the role of pre-verbal trauma that is nonetheless clinically significant in somatic and attachment-focused therapies.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists using EMDR, schema therapy, or CPT who conduct detailed trauma histories and ACE-informed case formulations

title: Is Employment Status Associated with Baseline Symptoms, Engagement, and Outcomes in Naturalistic Psychotherapy? Evaluation in a Large Community Mental Health Agency
authors: Zishan Jiwani, Qiang Xie, John J. Curtin, Kevin Riordan, Robbie Babins-Wagner, Derek Caperton, Mindi Thompson, Simon Goldberg
summary: In a large naturalistic study of 27,258 patients at a community mental health agency, this investigation examines how different employment statuses (employed, unemployed, disabled, student, retired) relate to baseline symptom severity, therapy engagement (session attendance, dropout), and treatment outcomes. The unusually large sample allows for granular employment categorization beyond the typical employed vs. unemployed dichotomy, revealing nuanced associations that smaller studies cannot detect.
clinical_insight: With 27,258 patients, this is one of the largest naturalistic psychotherapy studies to date, giving its findings exceptional ecological validity for community practice settings. For private practice clinicians, the key question is whether employment status functions as a moderator or mediator of treatment response. If unemployed patients show higher dropout, this has direct implications for engagement strategies at the outset of therapy. The tension between employment as a protective factor (structure, social contact, identity) versus a stressor (workplace toxicity, discrimination, accommodation needs) is particularly relevant for clients with personality disorders or chronic mental health conditions. A blind spot is that this study examines employment status categorically rather than assessing employment quality, which research increasingly shows is more clinically relevant than employment status alone for mental health outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinicians in community mental health and private practice who want to understand how socioeconomic factors like employment shape treatment engagement and outcomes

title: Interoceptive Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Attention are Associated with Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors
summary: This study of 450 adults examines three dimensions of interoception (accuracy, sensitivity, and attention) in relation to suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Individuals with recent suicidal thoughts and behaviors showed lower interoceptive accuracy, difficulty perceiving lower-intensity body sensations, and heightened attention to internal signals. These interoceptive differences persisted after controlling for depression and anxiety, suggesting they represent a distinct risk pathway for suicidality.
clinical_insight: The identification of interoceptive dysfunction as an independent risk factor for suicidality, separate from depression and anxiety, opens a new assessment and intervention pathway. For therapists conducting suicide risk assessments, incorporating brief interoceptive measures could improve risk stratification. For body-based therapists using somatic therapies, sensorimotor psychotherapy, or yoga-informed approaches, this provides empirical support for interventions that rebuild interoceptive awareness as a suicide prevention strategy. The tension between interoceptive accuracy (objective performance) and interoceptive sensitivity (subjective awareness) is clinically important: some patients may be highly attuned to body sensations but misinterpret them catastrophically, a pattern that is particularly relevant to panic disorder and health anxiety comorbid with suicidality. A blind spot is the cross-sectional design, which cannot establish whether interoceptive deficits precede suicidality or result from it, limiting causal inferences for treatment planning.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting suicide risk assessments and those using body-based or somatic approaches who want to understand the interoceptive dimensions of suicidal risk

title: Temporal Relations Among Changes in Activation, Avoidance, and Anxiety  in the Unified Protocol
authors: Doug Terrill, Matthew W. Southward, Madeline Kushner, Lindsey Crowley, Riley Feddock, Shannon Sauer-Zavala
summary: Using random intercept cross-lagged panel models, this study examines the temporal dynamics between behavioral activation, experiential avoidance, and anxiety symptoms during outpatient administration of the Unified Protocol (UP), a transdiagnostic CBT-based treatment. The study tests whether decreases in avoidance drive increases in activation, whether increases in activation drive decreases in anxiety, or whether these changes occur simultaneously, informing the proposed mechanisms of change in transdiagnostic treatments.
clinical_insight: Understanding the temporal sequence of change mechanisms in transdiagnostic treatments like the Unified Protocol has direct implications for session pacing and treatment planning. If avoidance reduction precedes behavioral activation, clinicians should prioritize exposure and avoidance reduction early in treatment before pushing for behavioral activation. Conversely, if activation drives avoidance reduction, then scheduling pleasurable activities first may create momentum that makes exposure work more tolerable. The Unified Protocol is increasingly used in private practice for its flexibility across anxiety, depression, and OCD presentations, yet its proposed mechanisms of change have not been thoroughly tested. This study begins to close that gap. A blind spot is that session-by-session mechanisms may differ from week-by-week mechanisms, and the aggregated data used here may mask important within-session processes that clinicians observe directly, such as the emotional processing that occurs during exposure exercises.
relevant_for: Clinicians using the Unified Protocol, transdiagnostic CBT, or any emotion-focused therapy where activation and avoidance are central treatment targets

title: Use of substances to cope predicts PTSD symptom persistence: Investigating patterns of interactions between PTSD symptoms and its maintaining mechanisms
authors: René Freichel, Nora Skjerdingstad, Alessandra Chiara Mansueto, Sacha Epskamp, Asle Hoffart, Sverre Urnes Johnson, Omid V. Ebrahimi
summary: This study investigates how substance use as a coping strategy interacts with established PTSD maintaining mechanisms, including cognitive and experiential avoidance, positive and negative metacognitions, to predict PTSD symptom persistence over time. Using network analysis, the research identifies substance use coping as a central node that interacts with multiple maintaining factors, suggesting it plays a unique role in perpetuating PTSD that goes beyond simple comorbidity.
clinical_insight: The finding that substance use coping occupies a central position in the network of PTSD-maintaining mechanisms has significant implications for treatment sequencing. For trauma therapists, this supports an integrated approach that addresses substance use coping early in treatment rather than treating PTSD and substance use as separate sequential conditions. The network approach reveals that substance use coping does not simply co-occur with PTSD but actively interacts with avoidance and metacognitive processes to maintain symptoms. For clinicians using PE or CPT, this means that untreated substance use coping may undermine exposure work by providing an alternative avoidance pathway. The tension in the literature between integrated vs. sequential treatment for comorbid PTSD and substance use remains unresolved; this study provides network-level evidence favoring integrated approaches. A blind spot is the absence of trauma type differentiation, as substance use coping may play different roles in combat-related PTSD versus interpersonal trauma, which has direct implications for treatment planning in private practice where interpersonal trauma predominates.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists working with clients who use substances to cope, particularly those using PE, CPT, or Seeking Safety-informed approaches

title: Unguided online treatment of disturbed grief, posttraumatic stress, and depression in adults bereaved during the COVID-19 pandemic: a randomized controlled trial
authors: Lyanne Reitsma, Paul A. Boelen, Jos de Keijser, Lonneke I.M. Lenferink
summary: This RCT evaluates an unguided online grief-specific CBT intervention compared to waitlist controls in 65 Dutch adults bereaved during the COVID-19 pandemic, targeting persistent complex bereavement disorder, PTSD, and depression symptoms. The study is the first to examine short-term effects of unguided online grief CBT, testing whether a fully automated intervention without therapist contact can reduce bereavement-related psychopathology.
clinical_insight: Unguided online grief CBT represents a potentially scalable approach to bereavement care, particularly relevant given the massive increase in pandemic-related loss. For private practice clinicians, the question is not whether to replace therapy with apps but how to integrate these tools into stepped care models. This study shows that even without therapist guidance, structured grief CBT produces meaningful symptom reduction for some bereaved individuals, which could free clinical resources for more complex cases. However, the RCT design with waitlist controls provides limited information about how unguided online grief CBT compares to therapist-guided treatment or in-person therapy, which is the clinically relevant comparison. The tension between accessibility and therapeutic depth is central: unguided interventions may reach more people but may fail those with complicated grief featuring traumatic loss, absent grieving, or identity disruption. A blind spot is the absence of longer-term follow-up, leaving it unclear whether unguided grief CBT produces durable change or merely accelerates natural recovery processes.
relevant_for: Grief therapists and clinicians working with bereaved populations who are considering integrating digital tools into their practice or stepped care models

title: Cognitive Rigidity, Habitual Tendencies, and Obsessive-Compulsive Symptoms: Individual Differences and Compensatory Interactions
authors: Smriti Ramakrishnan, Trevor William Robbins, Leor Zmigrod
summary: This study examines cognitive rigidity and habitual tendencies as interacting risk factors for OCD symptoms, testing whether these traits compensate for or amplify each other. Using an interactionist approach, the research investigates whether individuals high in cognitive rigidity but low in habitual tendencies show different OCD profiles than those high in both, and whether compensatory mechanisms exist that moderate the expression of obsessive-compulsive symptoms in daily life.
clinical_insight: The interactionist approach to understanding OCD moves beyond single-deficit models and has direct implications for case formulation. If cognitive rigidity and habitual tendencies interact rather than simply add up, then OCD treatment needs to address both simultaneously rather than targeting one at a time. For ERP therapists, this suggests that patients with high cognitive rigidity may resist exposure exercises not because of anxiety per se but because of difficulty shifting cognitive sets, requiring more psychoeducation and preparatory work before exposure can be effective. The compensatory interaction finding, if confirmed, would support a more personalized approach: patients high in rigidity but low in habitual tendencies may benefit more from cognitive therapy targeting inflexible beliefs, while those high in both may need combined ERP and cognitive interventions. The tension between cognitive and behavioral models of OCD, which has persisted for decades, is partially addressed by this interactionist framework. A blind spot is the reliance on non-clinical samples, which limits direct application to clinically diagnosed OCD patients who typically show more severe and entrenched symptoms.
relevant_for: OCD clinicians using ERP, cognitive therapy, or combined approaches who want to understand the interplay between cognitive and habit-based mechanisms

title: Emotional awareness, affective dysregulation, and bipolar spectrum psychopathology: A multilevel path analysis
authors: Sarah Havens Sperry, Nathaniel Eckland, Thomas Kwapil
summary: This study uses multilevel path analysis to examine how emotional awareness, specifically emotional clarity and attention to emotion, relates to affective dysregulation and bipolar spectrum psychopathology. Findings indicate that deficits in emotional clarity are associated with both affective dysregulation and bipolar spectrum symptoms, while excessive attention to emotion may amplify rather than mitigate mood instability, challenging the assumption that all forms of emotional awareness are beneficial.
clinical_insight: The nuanced finding that attention to emotion can be detrimental in bipolar spectrum conditions challenges a core assumption of many mindfulness-based and emotion-focused therapies. For clinicians treating bipolar disorder or subsyndromal bipolar presentations, this suggests that teaching clients to pay more attention to their emotions may be counterproductive if emotional clarity is not simultaneously developed. This has direct implications for MBCT, DBT skills groups, and ACT with bipolar clients where mindfulness of emotions is a standard component. The path analysis approach clarifies the sequence: poor emotional clarity leads to affective dysregulation, which in turn drives bipolar symptoms, suggesting that interventions should prioritize building emotional vocabulary and differentiation skills before introducing mindfulness or exposure-based emotion approaches. A blind spot is the focus on bipolar spectrum psychopathology rather than diagnosed bipolar disorder, meaning these findings may not generalize to patients with full-blown bipolar I disorder where mood episodes are more severe and less amenable to self-regulation strategies alone.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating bipolar spectrum conditions using CBT, MBCT, DBT, or psychoeducation approaches that incorporate emotional awareness components

title: Personality Markers for Common Mental Disorders: A Multi-Trait, Multi-Diagnosis, Multi-Rater Approach
authors: Ye Zhang, Michelle Luciano, Uku Vainik, René Mõttus
summary: Using data from the Estonian Biobank (N=3,221), this study identifies facet-level personality markers that predict common mental disorders including anxiety and mood disorders, going beyond broad trait domains to examine specific personality facets. By employing a multi-trait, multi-diagnosis, and multi-rater design, the research reveals that narrow personality facets substantially outperform broad domains in predicting clinical outcomes, with different facets predicting different disorders.
clinical_insight: The finding that narrow personality facets outperform broad traits for clinical prediction has significant implications for intake assessment in private practice. Rather than relying solely on broad measures like the NEO-FFI, clinicians should consider facet-level assessment tools like the NEO-PI-R or IPIP-300 to identify specific risk profiles. For example, a patient scoring high on vulnerability (a neuroticism facet) but low on impulsiveness may have a different prognosis and treatment trajectory than a patient with the same overall neuroticism score but the reverse facet profile. The tension between assessment efficiency (short broad measures) and predictive precision (long facet measures) is a practical dilemma for time-limited intake sessions. A blind spot is that biobank samples, while large, may underrepresent the severity and comorbidity typical of clinical populations, potentially overestimating how cleanly personality facets map onto single diagnoses.
relevant_for: Clinicians conducting personality-informed case formulations and intake assessments for anxiety and mood disorders in adult private practice

title: Temporal Relationships between Mood and Eating across Eating Disorders: A Network Approach
authors: Sercan Kahveci, Julia Reichenberger, Ann-Kathrin Arend, Alessandra Mansueto, René Freichel, Ulrich Voderholzer, Jens Blechert
summary: This network analysis examines prospective temporal relationships between negative and positive emotions and eating-related behaviors (hunger, food craving, calorie intake, binge episodes) across different eating disorder diagnoses and healthy controls. By mapping directional pathways between mood states and eating behaviors, the study tests whether the theorized vicious cycle between emotional distress and disordered eating operates consistently across AN, BN, BED, and healthy populations.
clinical_insight: Network analysis of temporal mood-eating relationships provides clinicians with a mechanistic understanding of when and how emotional interventions should be deployed in eating disorder treatment. If negative mood prospectively predicts binge episodes but not restriction, this supports prioritizing emotion regulation skills early in BN and BED treatment, while focusing on different mechanisms in AN. For CBT-E and DBT-ED practitioners, these network structures could guide individualized treatment planning by identifying each patient's specific mood-eating cycle. The tension between universal treatment protocols (CBT-E for all EDs) and diagnosis-specific mechanisms revealed by network analysis is significant: if the temporal structures differ across diagnoses, a one-size-fits-all approach may miss critical intervention targets. A blind spot is that network analyses capture population-level temporal patterns that may not apply to any individual patient, highlighting the ongoing gap between group-level network findings and idiographic clinical formulation.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating eating disorders with CBT-E, DBT-ED, or integrative approaches who want to understand the temporal dynamics of mood-eating relationships

title: Understanding Types of Transitions in Clinical Change: An Introduction from the Complex Dynamic Systems Perspective
authors: Jingmeng Cui, Fred Hasselman, Merlijn Olthof, Anna Lichtwarck-Aschoff
summary: This theoretical paper introduces four types of sudden transitions in clinical change drawn from complex dynamic systems theory: bifurcation-induced tipping, noise-induced tipping, rate-induced tipping, and noise-induced diffusion. Each transition type describes a distinct mechanism by which therapeutic change can occur suddenly rather than gradually, providing a unifying framework for understanding nonlinear change processes in psychotherapy.
clinical_insight: Sudden therapeutic change, long observed in clinical practice but poorly explained by linear models of psychotherapy, is a phenomenon most therapists recognize intuitively. This framework provides clinicians with a vocabulary for understanding why some sessions appear to produce breakthrough moments while others do not. B-tipping (a critical threshold crossed through accumulated change) supports the dose-response model of therapy where persistence matters. N-tipping (random fluctuations triggering change) helps explain why the same intervention works in one session but not another. For therapists, this validates the clinical intuition that some change cannot be planned but can be facilitated by creating conditions that make tipping points more likely. The tension between this framework and manualized approaches is that manualization assumes predictable dose-response relationships, while tipping point dynamics are inherently unpredictable. A blind spot is the absence of empirical validation of these transition types in actual therapy data.
relevant_for: Psychotherapists across all modalities interested in understanding and facilitating sudden therapeutic change and nonlinear recovery processes

title: Excessive switching in OCD and paranoia arises from different deficits in belief-updating
authors: Philip R. Corlett, Charlotte Freeland, Santiago Castiello de Obeso, Praveen Suthaharan, Christopher Pittenger
summary: Using a probabilistic reversal learning task, this study demonstrates that excessive belief switching in OCD and paranoia arises from fundamentally different cognitive deficits. Individuals with OCD show instability in learning from positive feedback, while those with paranoia show instability in learning from negative feedback. These distinct computational signatures suggest that OCD and paranoia, despite sharing surface-level features of cognitive inflexibility, are maintained by different belief-updating mechanisms.
clinical_insight: The identification of distinct belief-updating signatures for OCD versus paranoia has direct implications for differential diagnosis and treatment planning. In clinical practice, OCD and paranoid ideation can present similarly, particularly when OCD involves harm-related obsessions that resemble paranoid fears. This computational approach provides an objective framework for distinguishing them: OCD patients may be more responsive to interventions targeting positive feedback processing (e.g., behavioral experiments that disconfirm catastrophic predictions), while paranoid patients may need interventions targeting negative feedback processing (e.g., cognitive restructuring of threat beliefs). A blind spot is that the study does not examine how these computational signatures change with treatment, leaving it unclear whether successful therapy normalizes the specific deficit or works through compensatory mechanisms.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD and psychosis-spectrum presentations who need to differentiate between obsessive and paranoid cognitive patterns

title: Putting the Vicious Cycle to the Test: Evidence for the Cognitive Behavioral Model of Persistent Somatic Symptoms from an Online Study
authors: Alexander Hans Josef Sahm, Michael Witthöft, Josef Bailer, Daniela Mier
summary: This study tests the complete cognitive-behavioral model of persistent somatic symptoms using an online survey of 2,114 participants. Rather than examining isolated components, the research evaluates the full vicious cycle model including predisposing, perpetuating, and precipitating factors and their interactions in explaining somatic symptom onset and endurance. Results provide evidence for the model's proposed pathways linking illness-related cognition, health anxiety, and attentional focus to symptom persistence.
clinical_insight: The CBT model of persistent somatic symptoms is widely used in clinical practice but has rarely been tested in its entirety. This large-scale validation provides empirical support for the complete model, giving CBT practitioners stronger footing when explaining treatment rationale to skeptical patients who attribute symptoms purely to physical causes. For therapists working with FND, somatic symptom disorder, or illness anxiety, this supports a formulation-based approach mapping specific perpetuating factors. The tension between this model and the emerging neurobiological understanding of FND is significant: patients and some neurologists may view psychological models as dismissive, making psychoeducation a critical early intervention. A blind spot is the COVID-19 pandemic data collection, which may have inflated health anxiety and somatic symptom reporting.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners working with somatic symptom disorder, FND, illness anxiety, and chronic pain where the cognitive-behavioral vicious cycle model informs treatment

title: Pain before, during, and after non-suicidal self-injury: Findings from a large web study
authors: Ryan Carpenter, Johanna Hepp, Timothy J Trull
summary: In a large online survey of 1,630 individuals with NSSI histories, this study tests competing models of the role of pain in self-injury by examining pain before, during, and after NSSI acts. The research assesses pain experience descriptively and tests pre-registered hypotheses about factors explaining individual differences in NSSI pain, including dissociation, emotion regulation difficulties, and self-criticism, providing a comprehensive test of pain-onset, pain-offset, and analgesia models of NSSI.
clinical_insight: The competing models of pain in NSSI (pain as antecedent, consequence, or absent through analgesia) have direct implications for treatment. If pain onset drives NSSI, interventions should target the pre-existing emotional or physical pain preceding self-injury. If pain offset is the mechanism, treatment should focus on building alternative ways to achieve emotional relief. If analgesia is present, the task is to address dissociative processes that disconnect the person from their body. For DBT therapists teaching distress tolerance, understanding which pain model applies to each client determines whether to emphasize TIPP, alternative activities, or mindfulness to reduce dissociation. The tension between these models has persisted because most studies test only one. A blind spot is the reliance on retrospective self-report, which may be distorted by emotional state and time elapsed since episodes.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with NSSI in the context of BPD, complex trauma, or eating disorders, particularly those using DBT and schema therapy

title: Evolution of linguistic markers of agency, centrality and content during metacognitive therapy for psychosis
authors: Amir H. Nikzad, Paul H. Lysaker, Kyle S. Minor, Bethany Leonhardt, Mark Y. Liberman, Jenifer Vohs, Courtney N. Wiesepape, Sunny X. Tang
summary: This study examines how linguistic markers of agency, self-centrality, and thematic content evolve over the course of Metacognitive Reflection and Insight Therapy (MERIT) for psychosis. Using natural language processing to analyze therapy transcripts, the research tracks whether increases in metacognitive capacity are reflected in changes in how patients talk about themselves, providing an objective measure of recovery-oriented change in psychosis treatment.
clinical_insight: The use of linguistic analysis as an objective measure of therapeutic change in psychosis is a novel methodological contribution. For therapists using MERIT, metacognitive therapy, or recovery-oriented approaches to psychosis, the finding that linguistic markers of agency shift during treatment provides a concrete way to track progress beyond symptom reduction. If patients begin using more agentic language and less first-person centrality, this may indicate metacognitive capacity is developing before symptom changes appear. The tension between symptom-focused and recovery-focused outcomes in psychosis treatment is well-established, and this study provides tools for measuring the latter. A blind spot is that linguistic markers may reflect therapist influence or social desirability rather than genuine metacognitive change.
relevant_for: Therapists using MERIT, metacognitive therapy, or CBTp for psychosis who want to track recovery-oriented changes beyond symptom reduction

title: The Relationship Between the Perception of Major Life Events and Depression in Adulthood: A Systematic Scoping Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Peter Haehner, Felix Würtz, Sarah Kritzler, Marius Kunna, Maike Luhmann, Marcella L. Woud
summary: This systematic scoping review and meta-analysis synthesizes 276 studies (N=89,600) examining the relationship between how people perceive major life events and depression in adulthood. Rather than focusing on objective event occurrence, this review examines whether subjective perception, interpretation, and meaning-making around life events predicts depression onset, testing predictions from cognitive, stress-vulnerability, and rumination models.
clinical_insight: The massive scale of this meta-analysis (276 studies, nearly 90,000 participants) gives exceptional weight to its central finding: event perception matters as much as or more than event occurrence for depression risk. For CBT and schema therapy practitioners, this validates the core assumption that cognitive appraisal and meaning-making, rather than circumstances alone, drive depressive episodes. This supports interventions targeting cognitive reappraisal, schema modification, and narrative reconstruction of life events. The tension between changing circumstances and changing perceptions is fundamental: clients often want circumstances to change, while therapy primarily changes their relationship to those circumstances. A blind spot is the aggregation of cross-sectional studies where causality cannot be established, meaning depression may influence event perception rather than vice versa, creating a self-reinforcing cycle that CBT aims to interrupt.
relevant_for: CBT and schema therapy clinicians working with depression who need evidence for the centrality of cognitive appraisal in case formulations

title: A SMART Approach to Personalized Care: Preliminary Data on How to Select and Sequence Skills in Transdiagnostic CBT
authors: Shannon Sauer-Zavala, Matthew W. Southward, Nicole Stumpp, Stephen Semcho, Caitlyn O. Hood, Anna Garlock, Alex Urs
summary: Using a Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial (SMART) design, this study tests two strategies for increasing transdiagnostic CBT efficiency: personalized skill sequences (adapting the order of skills based on early response) and personalized skill selections (choosing which skills to teach based on patient characteristics). With 70 participants with anxiety and depression, the research collects feasibility and acceptability data on whether adaptive treatment algorithms can improve outcomes while reducing the number of sessions needed.
