clinical_insight: The SMART design represents the gold standard for developing adaptive treatment algorithms, and its application to transdiagnostic CBT is both timely and practically important. For private practice clinicians, the question of which skills to teach in which order is a daily clinical decision that currently relies on intuition rather than evidence. If this research identifies algorithm-supported sequences that outperform clinician judgment, it could transform how transdiagnostic CBT is delivered. The tension between protocol fidelity and personalized treatment is at the heart of evidence-based practice, and SMART designs provide the tools to resolve it empirically. A blind spot is the small sample size (N=70), limiting statistical power for detecting differences between adaptive strategies, meaning this should be viewed as a proof of concept rather than definitive evidence.
relevant_for: CBT clinicians and clinical researchers interested in personalized treatment algorithms and adaptive intervention design for transdiagnostic treatment

title: Intentional dating: A framework for sustaining erotic vitality in long-term relationships
summary: This paper introduces "intentional dating," a structured clinical framework grounded in Self-Determination Theory, the Dual Control Model, and Self-Expansion Theory to counteract the well-documented decline of sexual desire in long-term relationships. The framework emphasizes agency, competence, and attunement as core pillars for sustaining erotic vitality, offering therapists a systematic alternative to ad hoc suggestions about date nights or novelty exercises that often fail to address the deeper relational dynamics at play.
clinical_insight: Couples therapists can use this framework to move beyond generic advice and instead structure interventions around three evidence-based mechanisms: enhancing autonomous sexual agency, building relational competence through graduated challenges, and fostering attunement through mindful presence. The model bridges the gap between sexual science and clinical practice by providing a clear rationale for why standard relationship enrichment often falls short and what specific therapeutic moves can restore desire. A notable blind spot is the lack of empirical validation in clinical samples — the framework is currently theory-derived and awaits outcome trials.
relevant_for: Couples therapists and sex therapists working with clients experiencing desire discrepancy or erotic disengagement in long-term relationships

title: Integrating Digital Health Data into Clinical Practice: Perspectives and Practical Recommendations
authors: Marilyn Piccirillo, Miriam Hehlmann, Julian Burger, Mila Hall, Saskia Scholten
summary: This paper provides practical guidance for psychotherapists interested in using ambulatory assessment (AA) data from smartphones and wearables to track symptoms, experiences, and therapy processes between sessions. Unlike existing resources written for researchers, this guide is specifically tailored for clinical practitioners, offering recommendations on data collection, interpretation, and integration into the therapeutic workflow to enhance assessment precision and patient engagement.
clinical_insight: Therapists in private practice can leverage AA data to overcome the well-known limitations of retrospective self-report in session, gaining access to real-time fluctuations in mood, anxiety, and behavioral patterns that inform more precise case conceptualization and treatment monitoring. The paper bridges an important gap between digital mental health research and frontline clinical application, though it does not fully address barriers such as cost, patient digital literacy, or data privacy concerns that may limit adoption in smaller practices. A practical strength is the focus on therapist-friendly interpretation rather than technical implementation.
relevant_for: Clinicians in adult private practice interested in integrating digital assessment tools into their therapeutic workflow

title: Testing Reciprocal Within-Person Changes in Aversive Reactions to Emotions and Skill Use in the Unified Protocol
authors: Hannah Croom, Matthew W. Southward, Shannon Sauer-Zavala
summary: Using ecological momentary assessment data from 70 adults with emotional disorders undergoing the Unified Protocol (UP) for transdiagnostic treatment, this study examined how within-person changes in aversive reactions to emotions relate to skill use during treatment. The findings reveal bidirectional relationships: as patients practice UP skills, their aversive reactions to negative emotions decrease, which in turn predicts further skill engagement, suggesting a reinforcing therapeutic cycle.
clinical_insight: This study provides empirical support for the core mechanism of the Unified Protocol — that reducing aversive reactions to emotions is not just an outcome but an active driver of continued skill use. For therapists delivering transdiagnostic treatments, the findings reinforce the importance of explicitly tracking patients’ emotional reactivity as a process measure rather than focusing solely on symptom reduction. The EMA methodology demonstrates how intensive longitudinal data can illuminate within-session and between-session processes that traditional pre-post designs miss. The sample was predominantly White and female, limiting generalizability to more diverse clinical populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians delivering the Unified Protocol or other transdiagnostic CBT approaches for anxiety and mood disorders

title: A Descriptive Report on the Psychotherapy Experiences of Adult Autistic Laypeople: Service Utilization, Therapeutic Process Familiarity and Preferences, Client-Therapist Factors, Perceptions of Harm, and Considerations for Those Without Experience
summary: In a mixed-methods study of 229 autistic adults, this paper explores psychotherapy service utilization, preferences for therapeutic style (measured via the Cooper-Norcross Inventory of Preferences), client-therapist relationship factors, and experiences of perceived harm in therapy. Most participants had some therapy experience and expressed preferences for therapist directivity, encouragement of emotional expression, and structured approaches. The study also considers the perspectives of autistic individuals who have never accessed therapy, highlighting barriers rooted in sensory, communication, and systemic factors.
clinical_insight: This is one of the largest studies to date on autistic adults’ therapy preferences, offering directly actionable guidance for neurotypical therapists working with neurodivergent clients. The finding that autistic clients prefer greater directivity and structure challenges some contemporary therapy trends toward non-directive approaches and suggests that therapists should assess and adapt their style rather than assume a one-size-fits-all therapeutic stance. The qualitative data on perceived harm is particularly important — it underscores how well-meaning therapeutic interventions (e.g., prolonged eye contact, open-ended emotional exploration) can be experienced as aversive by autistic clients. A limitation is the reliance on self-selected participants, which may not capture the experiences of autistic individuals with the greatest barriers to engaging with research.
relevant_for: Therapists working with adult autistic clients, particularly those in private practice who may lack specialized autism training

title: Potential Harms from Emphasizing Individual Factors Over Structural Factors in Cognitive Behavioral Therapy with Stigmatized Groups
authors: Isaac L. Ahuvia, Jessica L. Schleider
summary: This paper critically examines how standard CBT formulations, which emphasize individual cognitive and behavioral factors, may inadvertently pathologize or minimize the role of structural oppression (racism, homophobia, sexism) in the mental health of stigmatized groups. The authors argue that when CBT frames distress primarily as the product of maladaptive thoughts rather than rational responses to genuinely oppressive conditions, it risks reinforcing internalized stigma and undermining therapeutic trust with marginalized clients.
clinical_insight: For CBT practitioners working with marginalized populations, this paper raises essential questions about case formulation fidelity and cultural responsiveness. Rather than abandoning CBT, the authors advocate for integrating structural awareness into standard cognitive restructuring — for example, helping clients distinguish between genuinely distorted cognitions and accurate perceptions of discrimination. This tension between individual-level and structural-level explanations is one of the most significant debates in contemporary CBT, and clinicians who fail to address it risk iatrogenic effects. A blind spot is the lack of specific, manualized protocols for structurally-informed CBT, leaving practitioners with conceptual guidance but limited practical tools.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners working with racially, sexually, or otherwise marginalized clients in adult private practice

title: All Tied Up: Perceptions of BDSM and Kink for Mental Health Providers
summary: This paper examines mental health providers’ perceptions, knowledge, and biases regarding BDSM and kink practices. Despite the removal of BDSM from the DSM as a pathological diagnosis, many clinicians remain poorly informed about kink-related practices, leading to stigma, misdiagnosis, and therapeutic rupture when kink-identified clients seek services. The paper reviews the empirical literature on BDSM and mental health, finding no association between consensual kink involvement and psychopathology, and discusses the ethical implications of clinician bias.
clinical_insight: Clinicians in private practice are highly likely to encounter kink-identified clients, yet most receive minimal training in this area. This paper provides a concise, evidence-based corrective to persistent myths linking consensual BDSM to pathology, and highlights how pathologizing attitudes can drive clients to conceal important aspects of their lives, directly undermining the therapeutic relationship. The paper underscores the need for therapists to develop kink-affirming competencies as part of broader cultural humility training. A limitation is the focus on provider perceptions rather than client-reported outcomes of kink-affirmative versus non-affirmative therapy.
relevant_for: All clinicians in adult practice, particularly couples and sex therapists who work with clients who practice consensual kink or BDSM

title: Structure of Pathological Personality Traits through the Lens of the CAT-PD Model
authors: Whitney R. Ringwald, Leah Emery, Shereen Khoo, Lee Anna Clark, Yuliya Kotelnikova, Matthew D. Scalco, David Watson, Aidan G.C. Wright, Leonard Simms
summary: In a pre-registered study, this paper investigates the domain-level structure of the Comprehensive Assessment of Traits Relevant to Personality Disorders (CAT-PD), a dimensional trait measure with broader content coverage than existing instruments like the PID-5. Using large-scale data, the authors examine the latent structure of pathological personality traits and establish construct validity against external criteria, aiming to provide a more clinically useful alternative to categorical personality disorder diagnoses.
clinical_insight: As the field moves toward dimensional models of personality pathology (as reflected in DSM-5-AMPD and ICD-11), clinicians need reliable, well-validated trait measures for case formulation and treatment planning. The CAT-PD offers wider coverage of personality-relevant domains than the PID-5, potentially capturing clinically important variance that current tools miss. For therapists working with personality-disordered clients, a better trait assessment means more precise targeting of interventions — for example, distinguishing between negative affectivity and emotional lability as separate treatment targets. A limitation is that the CAT-PD is not yet widely adopted in clinical settings, and the paper does not directly compare treatment utility against existing measures.
relevant_for: Clinicians and assessors working with personality pathology who use or are transitioning to dimensional trait models for case formulation

title: A Systematic Review and Theoretical Framework of Stress Generation in Daily Life
authors: Peter Haehner, Rosalie Andrae, Wiebke Bleidorn
summary: This systematic review integrates research on person-environment transactions with the clinical stress generation literature to develop a novel process model of daily stress generation. Unlike traditional stress generation research that examines major life events over months or years, this framework focuses on how individuals with elevated neuroticism or psychopathology actively contribute to minor stressors in their daily lives through specific behavioral and interpersonal mechanisms, creating self-perpetuating cycles of distress.
clinical_insight: The daily process model of stress generation has direct implications for CBT and behavioral activation interventions, as it identifies specific, moment-level behavioral patterns that clinicians can target in session. Rather than addressing abstract ‘stress’ as a global construct, therapists can help clients recognize how their own behaviors — such as reassurance-seeking, conflict avoidance, or procrastination — generate the very stressors that maintain depression and anxiety. The review’s strength is its bridge between personality-level vulnerability (neuroticism) and observable daily behaviors, making the concept clinically tractable. A limitation is the reliance on EMA studies that may not capture the full complexity of real-world stress generation processes.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and behavioral activation practitioners working with depressed or anxious clients who exhibit self-reinforcing stress cycles

title: Unveiling the Dynamics of Narcissistic Fluctuations and the Affective Cycles in Narcissism
authors: Radosław Rogoza, Georg Krammer, Lidia Baran, Emanuel Jauk, Maria Flakus, Rossella Di Pierro, MARCO DI SARNO, Emma K. Stapp, Marcin Zajenkowski, Michael Dufner, Ramzi Fatfouta
summary: Using intensive longitudinal data (N = 199; 7,991 responses) and dynamic structural equation modeling, this study maps how three facets of narcissism — agentic, neurotic, and antagonistic — fluctuate in daily life and how these fluctuations relate to momentary affect. A key finding is that increases in antagonistic narcissism predicted subsequent increases in both agentic and neurotic narcissism, revealing cascading dynamics that may help explain the affective instability characteristic of narcissistic personality presentations.
clinical_insight: For therapists working with clients who present with narcissistic traits or narcissistic personality disorder, these findings challenge the common clinical tendency to view narcissism as a static, monolithic construct. The demonstration that antagonistic narcissism may act as a catalyst triggering both grandiose and vulnerable states offers a concrete framework for psychoeducation and for helping clients recognize their own state-dependent patterns. This is particularly relevant for schema therapy and psychodynamic approaches that already conceptualize narcissistic presentations as involving oscillating self-states. A significant blind spot is the use of a non-clinical student-adjacent sample, which may not capture the more severe and treatment-resistant presentations seen in clinical practice.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with narcissistic personality pathology, particularly those using schema therapy, psychodynamic, or transference-focused approaches

title: Efficacy and Working Mechanisms of a Go/No-Go Task-Based Inhibition Training in Smoking: A Randomized-Controlled Trial
authors: Franziska Motka, Charlotte Wittekind, Leonie Ascone, Simone Kühn
summary: This pre-registered RCT (N = 122) tested the efficacy of a Go/No-Go task-based inhibitory control training on tobacco use and craving in adult smokers. Participants were randomized to either substance-specific inhibition training or an active control condition. The study also investigated potential working mechanisms, examining whether changes in inhibitory control mediated reductions in smoking behavior, providing insight into whether cognitive training can serve as a viable adjunct to standard smoking cessation treatments.
clinical_insight: Inhibitory control training represents a low-cost, easily disseminable adjunct to standard smoking cessation protocols that clinicians can recommend to clients who struggle with impulse control. The RCT design and mechanism-focused analysis are methodological strengths that address the long-standing criticism that cognitive training effects are poorly understood. For therapists working with substance use disorders, these findings contribute to the growing evidence that targeting domain-general cognitive processes (inhibition) can complement domain-specific interventions (nicotine replacement, motivational interviewing). A limitation is the focus on a single substance (tobacco) and the absence of long-term follow-up data beyond the immediate training period.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with tobacco dependence and substance use disorders who are interested in evidence-based adjunctive cognitive training interventions

title: Development of a training programme in individual case formulation skills and a scale for assessing its effectiveness
authors: Richard Hallam, Gary Brown, Liza Turner, Elizabeth Blomfield, Sharif El Leithy, Ines Mendes
summary: This paper develops and validates a structured training programme designed to improve psychotherapists’ individual case formulation skills — a core competency that bridges assessment and treatment planning but has historically lacked formal training frameworks. The authors also created a measurement scale to assess formulation competence, addressing a significant gap in psychotherapy training and supervision.
clinical_insight: Case formulation is the bridge between assessment data and treatment planning, yet most training programmes leave it to implicit learning through supervision. This paper offers a structured approach that could be integrated into practicum training, clinical supervision, and continuing education, particularly for CBT and integrative therapists. A limitation is that the training and scale are newly developed and await broader replication across therapeutic orientations beyond the authors’ primary modality.
relevant_for: Clinical supervisors and training programme directors seeking structured approaches to teach case formulation skills

title: Longitudinal Associations between Mindfulness and Change in Attachment Orientations in Couples: The Role of Relationship Preoccupation and Empathy
authors: Taranah Gazder, Sarah C. E. Stanton
summary: Using a longitudinal design, this study examined how general and relationship-specific mindfulness predict changes in attachment orientations (security, anxiety, avoidance) in adult couples over time. The findings suggest that mindfulness may enhance attachment security through mechanisms involving reduced relationship preoccupation and increased empathic responsiveness, offering an interpersonal pathway by which mindfulness-based interventions benefit couples.
clinical_insight: For couples therapists, this study provides empirical support for integrating mindfulness practices into couples work as a means of shifting underlying attachment dynamics rather than merely teaching communication skills. The finding that relationship-specific mindfulness (present-moment awareness during interactions with one’s partner) drives attachment security more than general mindfulness has direct implications for how therapists structure in-session mindfulness exercises. A blind spot is the lack of diversity reporting and the exclusive focus on mixed-gender couples, limiting generalisability to same-sex couples.
relevant_for: Couples therapists integrating mindfulness-based approaches or working with attachment-related relationship distress

title: Perceived responsiveness in suicidal ideation: an experience sampling study in psychiatric patients
authors: Laura Sels, Stephanie Homan, Harry Reis, Andrea B Horn, Jordan Revol, Urte Scholz, Tobias Kowatsch, Birgit Kleim
summary: Using ecological momentary assessment (EMA) in a sample of psychiatric patients, this study investigated whether momentary fluctuations in perceived responsiveness from close others — feeling understood, validated, and cared for — were associated with reductions in suicidal ideation within the same day. The EMA design captures the dynamic, real-time interplay between an interpersonal protective factor and suicidal thoughts that cross-sectional designs miss.
clinical_insight: The experience sampling methodology provides compelling evidence that perceived responsiveness operates as a within-day protective factor against suicidal ideation, supporting interpersonal theories of suicide (e.g., Joiner’s interpersonal theory). For clinicians, this reinforces the importance of explicitly strengthening patients’ perceived social support and responsiveness in therapy, and suggests that momentary assessments of connectedness could serve as early warning signals. The study’s use of EMA data from actual psychiatric patients strengthens ecological validity compared to student samples, though the correlational design precludes causal conclusions about the direction of responsiveness-ideation effects.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with suicidal patients in inpatient or outpatient psychiatric settings who want to leverage interpersonal factors in safety planning

title: Transdiagnostic cognitive biases in psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and network meta-analysis
authors: Katie M. Lavigne, Jiaxuan Deng, Delphine Raucher-Chéné, Adèle Hotte-Meunier, Chloé Voyer, Lisa Sarraf, Martin Lepage, Genevieve Sauve
summary: This systematic review and network meta-analysis examines cognitive biases — systematic tendencies in attention, memory, and interpretation — across multiple psychiatric disorders, testing whether these biases share common underlying mechanisms. The network approach maps how different biases relate to each other and to symptom severity across diagnostic categories, offering a data-driven taxonomy of cognitive dysfunction.
clinical_insight: The transdiagnostic perspective on cognitive biases has direct implications for case formulation and treatment planning across diagnostic boundaries. If biases like attentional threat bias, interpretation bias, and memory bias form interconnected networks, then targeting a central bias node could produce cascading improvements across multiple symptom domains. This supports the rationale for transdiagnostic treatments like the Unified Protocol that address cognitive processing broadly rather than disorder-specific content. The meta-analytic approach is methodologically strong, but the review is limited by the heterogeneity of bias measures across studies and the predominance of anxiety and depression samples.
relevant_for: Clinicians using transdiagnostic approaches (UP, CBT-E, DBT) who want to understand which cognitive biases to prioritize in treatment

title: The impact of a remote digital health intervention for anxiety and depression on occupational and functional impairment: an observational, pre-post intervention study
authors: Marcos Economides, Kristian Ranta, Outi Hilgert, Dolores Kelleher, Patricia Arean, Valerie Forman Hoffman
summary: This pre-post observational study evaluated the Meru Health Program, a structured, self-guided smartphone-based intervention with asynchronous therapist support, in working adults with anxiety and/or depression. The study specifically examined whether the intervention improved not only symptoms but also occupational impairment and daily functioning, addressing a critical gap in the digital mental health literature where functional outcomes are often neglected.
clinical_insight: Digital mental health interventions are increasingly offered to working adults through employee assistance programmes and insurance plans, but evidence on their real-world effectiveness for functional outcomes remains limited. This study provides support for the practical utility of guided digital interventions in reducing occupational impairment, not just symptom scores. For private practice therapists, understanding which digital tools have evidence for functional recovery helps in making informed referrals and in integrating digital adjuncts into treatment plans. A limitation is the pre-post design without a control group, which cannot rule out placebo or time effects.
relevant_for: Clinicians in private practice who refer clients to or integrate digital mental health tools alongside face-to-face therapy for anxiety and depression

title: Specifying the Mechanisms and Targets of Emotion Regulation: A Translational Framework from Affective Science to Psychological Treatment
authors: Matthew W. Southward, Shannon Sauer-Zavala, Jennifer S. Cheavens
summary: This paper proposes a translational framework that bridges affective science research on emotion regulation with clinical treatment practice. Rather than teaching clients a broad repertoire of regulation strategies, the authors argue for specifying the precise mechanisms (e.g., attentional deployment, cognitive change, response modulation) and targets (e.g., specific emotions, contexts, or dysregulation patterns) that are most relevant to each individual, making emotion regulation training more efficient and precisely targeted.
clinical_insight: This framework directly addresses a common clinical challenge: treatment manuals often prescribe a wide range of emotion regulation strategies without specifying which ones will be most impactful for a given client. By mapping affective science mechanisms onto clinical targets, therapists can make more precise formulation-driven decisions about which strategies to prioritise. This is particularly relevant for transdiagnostic treatments like the Unified Protocol, DBT, and ACT. A limitation is that the framework is primarily conceptual and awaits empirical validation through treatment outcome studies that test mechanism-target matching versus standard broad-spectrum approaches.
relevant_for: Therapists delivering transdiagnostic emotion regulation treatments who want to move from one-size-fits-all manuals to formulation-driven strategy selection

title: An Attachment Perspective on the Risk for Psychosis: Clinical Correlates and the Predictive Value of Attachment Patterns and Mentalization
authors: Tommaso Boldrini, Maria Pontillo, Annalisa Tanzilli, Guido Giovanardi, Giuseppe Di Cicilia, Silvia Salcuni, Stefano Vicari, Vittorio Lingiardi
summary: In a longitudinal design, this study examined the nature of attachment patterns and reflective functioning (mentalization) in individuals at ultra-high risk (UHR) for psychosis, and tested the predictive value of attachment and mentalization for subsequent transition to psychosis. The study found that insecure attachment and impaired mentalization were associated with more severe subclinical psychotic symptoms.
clinical_insight: This study contributes to the growing evidence that psychosis risk is not solely determined by neurobiological factors but also by interpersonal developmental experiences. For clinicians working with early psychosis or UHR populations, assessing attachment patterns and mentalization capacity can enrich case formulation and inform treatment approaches that target interpersonal functioning. The longitudinal design with transition-to-psychosis outcomes is a significant methodological strength. A limitation is that the UHR sample size limits statistical power for predicting the relatively rare transition outcome.
relevant_for: Clinicians working in early intervention psychosis services who want to integrate attachment and mentalization assessments into their formulation and treatment planning

title: Is physical activity associated with lower borderline personality disorder severity, accounting for insomnia disorder?
authors: Valentin Krieger, Samuel St-Amour, Paquito Bernard, Niharika Parashar, Matthieu Samson, Lionel Cailhol
summary: Cross-sectional study (N=192) examining whether physical activity is associated with reduced BPD severity after accounting for comorbid insomnia disorder. Findings suggest physical activity may independently contribute to lower BPD symptom severity beyond its effects on sleep, supporting exercise as an adjunctive intervention in BPD treatment.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with BPD patients should systematically assess physical activity levels and sleep quality as modifiable treatment targets. The finding that exercise benefits BPD severity independently of insomnia suggests that prescribing physical activity alongside dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) or mentalization-based treatment (MBT) could address both affective dysregulation and behavioral impulsivity through non-pharmacological pathways. A notable limitation is the cross-sectional design, which cannot establish causation — exercise may simply be a marker of better overall functioning.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating borderline personality disorder in outpatient and partial hospitalization settings

title: Beyond Symptom Reduction:  The Causal Effect of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy on Interpersonal Problems
authors: Jonas Hallgans, Stephan Bartholdy, Tim Kaiser, Marit Kristine List, Eva-Lotta Brakemeier
summary: Examines the causal effect of routine outpatient CBT on reducing interpersonal problems using a quasi-experimental design comparing treated patients with a naturalistic waitlist control. The study finds that CBT produces meaningful reductions in interpersonal difficulties beyond general symptom improvement, with heterogeneous treatment effects across interpersonal problem subtypes.
clinical_insight: This study provides empirical support for what many CBT therapists observe clinically: that therapy changes not just symptoms but relational patterns. The finding of heterogeneous treatment effects means therapists should assess specific interpersonal profiles (e.g., intrusive vs. avoidant vs. vindictive) at intake to tailor interventions and set realistic expectations. A limitation is the quasi-experimental rather than randomized design, though the routine outpatient setting enhances ecological validity.
relevant_for: CBT therapists treating adults with comorbid mood and personality disorders in outpatient settings

title: USE OF MULTISENSORY INFORMATION AS FACILITATOR OF  GROUP SCHEMA THERAPY FOR BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER
authors: Tarja Koffert, Sinikka Luutonen, Jarmo Hietala
summary: Proposes a novel method for facilitating group schema therapy (GST) for BPD using patient-made video recordings watched collectively during sessions. The approach aims to reduce high drop-out rates and heterogeneous treatment responses by making schema modes more observable and tangible for both patients and therapists through multisensory information.
clinical_insight: Schema therapists running BPD groups should consider incorporating patient-generated video homework where patients record themselves in triggering situations and review them in-session. This technique makes abstract schema modes (e.g., Vulnerable Child, Detached Protector) directly observable, potentially accelerating schema mode recognition and limited reparenting. The main limitation is the theoretical proposal stage — controlled trials are needed before widespread adoption, and therapists must carefully manage potential shame and self-criticism triggered by video self-observation.
relevant_for: Schema therapists facilitating group therapy for borderline personality disorder

title: Mental Health and Intolerance of Uncertainty across Domains and Age: A Special Role for Intolerance of Social Uncertainty
authors: Weike Wang, Sarah Daniels, Susanne Schweizer
summary: Investigates whether intolerance of uncertainty (IU) operates similarly across different life domains (health, finance, social, environmental) and age groups in relation to mental health outcomes. Findings reveal that intolerance of social uncertainty shows a uniquely strong association with anxiety and depression, suggesting domain-specific pathways rather than a unitary IU construct.
clinical_insight: When assessing and treating anxiety disorders, clinicians should move beyond global IU measures and specifically probe intolerance of social uncertainty, which appears to be the most clinically potent domain. For patients with social anxiety, GAD, or health anxiety, differentiating the domain of uncertainty the patient struggles with can guide more precise exposure hierarchies and cognitive restructuring targets. The cross-sectional design limits causal inference, and the study does not include clinical samples exclusively, so findings need validation in treatment-seeking populations.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists treating generalized anxiety, social anxiety, and health anxiety disorders

title: Differences in metacognitive functioning between obsessive-compulsive disorder patients and highly compulsive individuals from the general population
authors: Monja Hoven, Marion Rouault, Ruth van Holst, Judy Luigjes
summary: Compares metacognitive functioning — specifically confidence judgments — between OCD patients and highly compulsive individuals from the general population without an OCD diagnosis. The study addresses a central debate in the OCD literature: whether obsessive doubts reflect abnormally low confidence (clinical model) or impaired confidence estimation and usage (cognitive model), finding evidence that distinguishes clinical from subclinical compulsivity.
clinical_insight: This study sharpens the differential between pathological OCD and subclinical compulsivity by identifying distinct metacognitive profiles. For therapists, this supports the use of metacognitive-focused interventions (e.g., targeting confidence calibration and belief ratings) specifically for OCD patients, while subclinical compulsive individuals may respond better to behavioral experiments that address estimation processes. Clinicians should note the critical blind spot: the study relies on laboratory confidence tasks that may not fully capture the phenomenological experience of real-world obsessive doubt.
relevant_for: ERP and metacognitive therapists treating obsessive-compulsive disorder

title: Unraveling individual patterns of passive and active suicidal ideation: A person-specific network modeling exploration
authors: Lara Kirchhofer, Ai Ye, Isaac Galatzer-Levy, Urte Scholz, Birgit Kleim, Stephanie Homan
summary: Uses person-specific network modeling of ecological momentary assessment data to examine how passive and active suicidal ideation differ at the individual level. While group-level networks capture average patterns, person-specific models reveal unique configurations of cognitive and emotional states that precede shifts between passive wish-to-die and active intent, highlighting the limitations of one-size-fits-all risk assessment.
clinical_insight: This study underscores why standardized suicide risk assessments often fail: the pathways from distress to suicidal ideation are individually configured. Therapists should collaboratively map each patient’s personal network of triggers (e.g., hopelessness leading to passive ideation vs. anger leading to active ideation) and use this formulation to guide safety planning. The person-specific approach aligns well with the CAMS framework. A key limitation is the intensive EMA design, which may not be feasible in routine clinical care, though the conceptual framework can still inform clinical interviews.
relevant_for: Suicide prevention clinicians and crisis therapists using EMA-informed formulation

title: Exploring the Link Between Hypersomnolence and Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: A Case Series.
authors: Vlad Bucurescu, Laure Peter-Derex, Maria Livia Fantini, Christophe Moderie, Benjamin PUTOIS
summary: Retrospective case series of 17 patients previously diagnosed with idiopathic hypersomnia who were reassessed and found to meet criteria for complex PTSD. The study highlights a possible association between hypersomnolence and trauma-related disorders, suggesting that excessive sleepiness may be an underrecognized symptom of CPTSD rather than a primary sleep disorder.
clinical_insight: Sleep medicine and trauma therapists should collaborate when patients present with unexplained hypersomnolence, as this may signal underlying CPTSD rather than a primary sleep disorder. For trauma therapists, screening for excessive daytime sleepiness could reveal a dissociative shutdown pattern that responds to phase-based trauma treatment (stabilization before processing). The case series design is inherently limited — it cannot establish causation or prevalence, and retrospective reassessment introduces diagnostic bias. However, it opens an important clinical question about whether sleep-focused interventions alone are insufficient for this subgroup.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and sleep medicine specialists assessing patients with unexplained hypersomnia

title: Beyond diagnostic artefact: structural stigma, epistemic injustice, and iatrogenic harm in the CPTSD–BPD debate (Response to D’agostino et al., 2026)
summary: A commentary arguing that the CPTSD-BPD diagnostic debate is incomplete when framed solely as a question of nosological overlap. Drawing on lived experience research and analyses of epistemic injustice, the paper demonstrates how the BPD label itself can produce structural stigma and iatrogenic harm, particularly for trauma survivors whose experiences are pathologized as personality dysfunction.
clinical_insight: Therapists should be mindful that the BPD diagnosis, while clinically useful for treatment planning, carries significant iatrogenic risks including self-stigma, invalidation of trauma narratives, and restrictive treatment access. For trauma-focused therapists, this commentary supports using formulation-based approaches (e.g., ICD-11 CPTSD framework) alongside or instead of BPD diagnoses, especially with complex trauma survivors. The blind spot is that this is a conceptual commentary rather than empirical research — therapists should balance these concerns with the practical reality that BPD diagnosis often unlocks insurance-funded treatment.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and clinical psychologists navigating the CPTSD vs. BPD diagnostic landscape

title: Confabulation and False Memory in Clinical Psychology: A Review of Theoretical Perspectives and Clinical Implications
summary: Reviews theoretical perspectives on confabulation and false memory, distinguishing their different aetiologies and clinical meanings. Confabulation typically follows neurological insult and reflects memory gap-filling, while false memories arise from source-monitoring errors and schema-driven reconstruction common in healthy cognition. The review synthesizes clinical implications for assessment and treatment across neuropsychological and psychotherapeutic contexts.
clinical_insight: Clinicians conducting trauma-focused therapy must distinguish between confabulation (suggesting neurological involvement requiring referral) and false memory (a normal cognitive process amplified by suggestive techniques). The review supports careful use of memory recovery techniques, emphasizing that source-monitoring training can help patients differentiate genuine memories from reconstructed ones. A key blind spot is the limited empirical guidance on how to apply these distinctions in real-time clinical sessions with trauma survivors reporting recovered memories.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and trauma therapists assessing memory accuracy in clinical practice

title: The Development of a Culturally-Adapted Dialectical Behavioral Therapy Single-Session Intervention: Encouraging Flexible Control
summary: Develops and describes a culturally-adapted DBT single-session intervention for African American women experiencing intersectional stress and overcontrolled coping patterns. The adaptation validates environmental stressors while exploring flexibility in coping, integrating cultural context into the DBT framework of dialectical thinking between acceptance and change.
clinical_insight: DBT therapists working with marginalized populations should consider how cultural stressors shape overcontrolled coping patterns and adapt skills training accordingly. The single-session format makes this intervention practical for crisis intervention, intake sessions, or settings where full DBT programs are unavailable. The main limitation is the early development stage without controlled outcome data, though the cultural adaptation framework provides a replicable model for other populations experiencing intersectional stress.
relevant_for: DBT therapists and crisis clinicians working with African American women and other marginalized groups

title: Self-Esteem and Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors: A Meta-Analytic Review
summary: Meta-analysis of 114 studies examining the association between self-esteem and suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Finds moderate-to-strong negative associations (r=-.435 for ideation, r=-.258 for behavior), with moderation by age, clinical status, and measurement approach. Results support self-esteem as both a risk marker and potential intervention target for suicide prevention.
clinical_insight: This meta-analysis provides robust evidence that self-esteem is a significant and modifiable risk factor for suicidal ideation and behavior, with effect sizes large enough to be clinically meaningful. Therapists should integrate self-esteem assessment into suicide risk formulations and consider interventions that directly target self-worth, such as compassion-focused therapy or schema therapy limited re-parenting, alongside standard safety planning. A critical blind spot is that most included studies are cross-sectional, limiting causal inference about whether low self-esteem causes suicidality or vice versa.
relevant_for: Suicide risk assessors and therapists using CFT or schema therapy with suicidal patients

title: Intolerance of Uncertainty and Psychosis: A Systematic Review
summary: Systematic review examining the relationship between intolerance of uncertainty (IU) and psychosis-spectrum symptoms. Finds evidence that IU is elevated in individuals with schizophrenia-spectrum diagnoses and contributes to distress, delusional conviction, and anomalous experiences, positioning IU as a transdiagnostic mechanism relevant to early intervention approaches for psychosis.
clinical_insight: For clinicians working with early psychosis or high-risk populations, assessing intolerance of uncertainty provides a transdiagnostic target that bridges anxiety and psychotic experiences. ACT-based interventions that build psychological flexibility in the face of uncertainty may reduce both distress and delusional conviction, complementing antipsychotic medication. The review highlights the tension between psychosis-specific and transdiagnostic models, noting that IU-based interventions developed for anxiety may need significant adaptation for psychotic populations. A limitation is the relatively small evidence base compared to the anxiety-IU literature.
relevant_for: Early psychosis clinicians and ACT therapists working with schizophrenia-spectrum disorders

title: Predictive Formats and Informational Attenuation: A Unified Account of Autism Across Cognition, Phenomenology, and Social Interaction
summary: Proposes a unified account of autism grounded in predictive processing and information theory, explaining autistic differences in perception, reasoning, and social interaction as systematic consequences of attenuated prior precision. The framework integrates cognitive, phenomenological, and social levels, offering a parsimonious alternative to fragmented domain-specific explanations of autistic cognition.
clinical_insight: This predictive processing framework gives therapists a coherent way to understand autistic clients experiences without pathologizing them: sensory sensitivities, social differences, and reliance on routine all follow from the same underlying mechanism of attenuated prior precision. For assessment, this supports moving away from deficit-based models toward understanding how information processing differences create specific strengths and challenges. The key limitation is that this remains a theoretical framework without direct clinical trial evidence, though it aligns with neurodiversity-affirming approaches gaining traction in clinical practice.
relevant_for: Neuropsychologists and therapists conducting autism assessments and neurodiversity-affirming therapy with adult autistic clients

title: Evidence that Tetris Reduces Immediate but Not Subsequent Daily Intrusions of a Trauma Film: A Multilab Replication Study
summary: A multilab replication (13 labs, N=739) tested whether playing Tetris after trauma film viewing reduces intrusive memories. Results confirmed Tetris reduces immediate intrusions but found no evidence it reduces subsequent daily intrusions, challenging the clinical promise of brief Tetris-based interventions for preventing PTSD symptom development after trauma exposure.
clinical_insight: The finding that Tetris reduces immediate but not daily intrusions tempers enthusiasm for using it as a standalone early intervention after trauma. Therapists working in acute settings should view Tetris as a potentially useful adjunct that may help in the immediate hours post-trauma but cannot replace comprehensive early interventions such as psychological first aid or trauma-focused CBT. This also highlights the gap between lab analog paradigms and real-world traumatic experiences, reminding clinicians that experimental findings require careful translation to clinical practice.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and crisis intervention workers in emergency and acute care settings

title: The Costs and Benefits of Mindfulness and Reappraisal in Daily Life
summary: An ecological momentary assessment study comparing mindfulness and cognitive reappraisal in daily life found that reappraisal was more effective at reducing negative affect but required more effort, while mindfulness had lower cost but weaker immediate emotion-change effects. The two strategies showed divergent profiles in their temporal dynamics and affective outcomes across daily experiences.
clinical_insight: This study provides evidence-based guidance for helping patients choose between mindfulness and reappraisal strategies in daily life rather than prescribing one uniformly. For patients with limited cognitive resources or those prone to rumination, mindfulness may be more sustainable long-term; for those needing rapid affect reduction in specific situations, reappraisal remains the stronger option. Therapists can use these differential cost-benefit profiles to support personalized emotion regulation strategy selection during skills training in CBT, DBT, or ACT.
relevant_for: CBT, DBT, and ACT therapists teaching emotion regulation skills to adults with mood and anxiety disorders

title: Effort Beats Effectiveness in Emotion Regulation Choice: Differences Between Suppression and Distancing
summary: Investigated whether people choose emotion regulation strategies based on their effectiveness or their perceived effort cost. Across subjective and physiological measures, participants preferred cognitive distancing over expressive suppression primarily because distancing felt less effortful, even when suppression was equally effective at reducing negative affect. The study highlights a systematic bias toward low-effort strategies in emotion regulation selection.
clinical_insight: Patients may avoid effective but effortful regulation strategies like cognitive restructuring in favor of easier but potentially less adaptive options like suppression or simple distraction. Clinicians should explicitly address effort perceptions during skills training, perhaps by scaffolding cognitive techniques gradually to reduce perceived difficulty, and by psychoeducating patients that the initial effort investment in strategies like distancing or reappraisal yields stronger and more durable emotion regulation benefits over time.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists working with patients who struggle to engage with effortful cognitive techniques

title: Reconceptualizing Attachment-Related Differences: From Proximity-Seeking to Epistemic Trust
summary: Proposes a reconceptualization of attachment theory that shifts focus from proximity-seeking behaviors to the development of epistemic trust—the capacity to trust interpersonal communication as relevant and meaningful. Drawing on Fonagy and colleagues’ work, the paper argues that early attachment experiences shape not just relationship patterns but fundamentally alter how individuals process and trust social information, with profound implications for understanding personality pathology and therapeutic change.
clinical_insight: Reframing attachment difficulties as disruptions in epistemic trust rather than merely proximity-seeking patterns gives therapists a new lens for understanding therapeutic resistance and ruptures in patients with personality disorders. When patients dismiss interpretations or seem unable to take in therapeutic input, this may reflect damaged epistemic trust from early attachment trauma rather than willful resistance. Mentalization-based treatment (MBT) and psychodynamic approaches that prioritize rebuilding trust in the therapist’s epistemic authority before interpretation can be particularly effective for these patients.
relevant_for: Psychodynamic and MBT therapists treating personality disorders and patients with chronic attachment trauma

title: Epistemic Trust and the Emergence of Conduct Problems: Aggression in the Service of Communication
summary: Extends the epistemic trust framework to understand conduct problems and aggression, proposing that aggressive behavior can be understood as a maladaptive attempt to establish communicative contact when epistemic trust has been compromised. The paper argues that conduct problems may serve a signaling function when individuals have learned that conventional communication is not reliably received or understood by caregivers.
clinical_insight: Understanding aggression as a communication strategy rooted in epistemic distrust can shift therapeutic approaches with oppositional and conduct-disordered presentations from purely behavioral contingency management toward relational repair. Therapists working with these patients should prioritize building a reliably responsive relationship before expecting behavioral change, recognizing that aggression may diminish naturally as the patient develops trust that their verbal and emotional communications will be received. A limitation is that the framework is primarily theoretical and does not yet have extensive empirical validation in clinical samples.
relevant_for: Therapists working with personality disorders, conduct problems, and oppositional presentations in adult clinical practice

title: Fear From Afar, Not So Risky After All: Distancing Moderates the Relationship Between Fear and Risk-Taking
summary: Demonstrates that cognitive distancing—a core reappraisal technique—effectively moderates the relationship between fear and risk-taking behavior. When participants adopted a distancing perspective, fear no longer predicted risk-taking, suggesting that reappraisal-based distancing can decouple emotional responses from maladaptive behavioral decisions.
clinical_insight: Provides experimental evidence that distancing techniques taught in CBT and ACT can effectively disrupt the fear-behavior chain that maintains anxiety disorders, particularly in situations where fear drives avoidance or impulsive risk-taking. Therapists can use this finding to strengthen the rationale for teaching distancing skills, showing patients that changing their relationship to fear thoughts rather than eliminating fear itself is sufficient to change behavioral outcomes. A blind spot is that the study used non-clinical samples, so the magnitude of distancing effects in clinical anxiety populations remains to be established.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT therapists treating anxiety disorders where fear-driven avoidance or risk-taking is a maintaining factor

title: Stability and Transition Likelihood of Primary Symptoms in Adults with Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A 5-Year Prospective Follow-Up Study
summary: A 5-year prospective follow-up study examining the stability and transition patterns of primary OCD symptoms in adults, tracking how symptom presentations change or persist over time. The study provides longitudinal data on which OCD symptom dimensions are most stable and which are most likely to transition, informing long-term treatment planning and prognosis.
clinical_insight: Understanding which OCD symptoms remain stable versus which tend to shift over a 5-year period helps therapists set realistic treatment expectations and plan for potential symptom migration. When patients present with changing OCD presentations years into treatment, this may reflect natural symptom transition rather than treatment failure, and clinicians should adapt intervention targets accordingly rather than assuming relapse. The finding that some symptom dimensions are highly stable reinforces the value of dimension-specific treatment planning using approaches like exposure and response prevention tailored to the dominant symptom profile.
relevant_for: OCD specialists and clinical psychologists providing long-term treatment for adult obsessive-compulsive disorder

title: A Functional Approach to Borderline Personality Disorder
summary: Proposes a functional analytic approach to understanding borderline personality disorder, examining BPD behaviors through the lens of their adaptive function within the individual's context rather than as pure pathology. The paper integrates functional behavioral analysis with contemporary BPD theory to offer a framework for understanding emotion dysregulation, interpersonal instability, and identity disturbance as context-dependent behavioral patterns serving identifiable functions.
clinical_insight: A functional approach invites therapists to ask not just 'what is wrong with this patient?' but 'what is this behavior accomplishing for them?' before intervening. This perspective can reduce therapeutic burnout by fostering curiosity about BPD behaviors rather than frustration, and supports more precisely targeted interventions — for example, addressing self-harm as an emotion regulation strategy before attempting to replace it, or understanding abandonment fears as functional responses to genuine interpersonal learning histories. The framework complements DBT and schema therapy by adding a behavioral function layer to existing treatment models.
relevant_for: DBT and schema therapy practitioners treating borderline personality disorder in adult outpatient settings

title: The Effects of Dialectical Behavior Therapy on Joy: A Diary Card Study
summary: A diary card study examining whether and how Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) increases experiences of positive emotion, specifically joy, in patients undergoing treatment. Using ecological momentary assessment via standard DBT diary cards, the study tracks changes in positive affect over the course of treatment, extending DBT outcome research beyond symptom reduction to include positive emotional functioning.
clinical_insight: DBT is often perceived as primarily focused on reducing self-harm, suicidality, and emotional dysregulation, but this study suggests it may also actively cultivate positive emotional experiences like joy. Therapists can use this finding to motivate patients who may view DBT as solely about crisis management, highlighting the potential for genuine improvements in quality of life and positive affect. The diary card methodology also demonstrates how routine DBT practice tools can serve dual purposes as both clinical monitoring and research instruments, supporting data-driven treatment adaptation.
relevant_for: DBT therapists and clinical program directors measuring treatment outcomes beyond symptom reduction in BPD and emotion dysregulation disorders

title: Use of Multisensory Information as Facilitator of Group Schema Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder
summary: This paper proposes a novel clinical method for enhancing group schema therapy (GST) for borderline personality disorder (BPD) by incorporating patient-recorded video home assignments. Patients film themselves at home following therapist instructions, then review the recordings in the group session to observe their own speech, gestures, postures, and facial expressions. These multisensory observations are used to examine and restructure dysfunctional emotion-cognition links, with the group providing corrective feedback. The authors present a clinical vignette illustrating the technique and argue that multisensory information facilitates cognitive restructuring more effectively than verbal report alone.
clinical_insight: High dropout rates in BPD group therapy remain a significant clinical challenge. This approach leverages embodied self-observation to help patients access maladaptive schemas through a concrete, multisensory channel rather than relying solely on verbal introspection — a method that may bypass the alexithymic and dissociative barriers common in BPD. Clinicians running schema therapy groups could integrate video-based home assignments to strengthen the experiential component of limited re-parenting and schema mode work. A notable limitation is the reliance on a single clinical vignette without controlled efficacy data.
relevant_for: Clinicians facilitating group schema therapy for adults with borderline personality disorder

title: Investigating the Efficacy of Group Schema Therapy on Perfectionism, Alexithymia, and Obsessive-Compulsive Personality Disorder Symptoms
summary: This quasi-experimental study examined the efficacy of 10 sessions of group schema therapy for individuals diagnosed with obsessive-compulsive personality disorder (OCPD; N=30, SCID-5-PD confirmed). Participants were randomly assigned to a schema therapy group or a no-intervention control. Group schema therapy significantly reduced OCPD symptoms and perfectionism (p < 0.05) as measured by the MCMI-III and Hill Perfectionism Questionnaire. However, alexithymia (TAS-20) did not show significant change, suggesting that while schema therapy can modify rigid cognitive-behavioral patterns in OCPD, the affective awareness deficits in this population may require additional or alternative intervention.
clinical_insight: OCPD is notoriously difficult to treat due to patients' rigid personality structure and limited insight into emotional processes. This study provides preliminary evidence that a brief 10-session group schema therapy protocol can reduce perfectionism and OCPD symptomatology — a promising finding given the scarcity of treatment trials for this disorder. The lack of change in alexithymia is clinically important: it suggests therapists should anticipate that cognitive restructuring alone may not unlock emotional awareness in OCPD and should consider integrating emotion-focused techniques such as experiential exercises or chair work. The small sample size and absence of follow-up data are limitations.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with adults with obsessive-compulsive personality disorder in group or individual therapy settings

title: Working with Dissociation in the Context of First Episode Psychosis: Guidance for Therapists
summary: This clinical guidance paper addresses the under-recognized phenomenon of dissociation in first-episode psychosis (FEP), focusing on a specific subtype termed 'felt sense of anomaly' dissociation. The authors present a cognitive-behavioural formulation where affect-related changes are interpreted as internal threats, driving a maintenance cycle of catastrophic appraisals and safety behaviours. The paper outlines assessment considerations, formulation frameworks, and evidence-based intervention techniques that can be integrated into existing CBT-for-psychosis protocols. Importantly, the guidance demonstrates that widely used therapeutic skills — including psychoeducation, behavioural experiments, and cognitive restructuring — can be effectively adapted to target dissociation within psychosis.
clinical_insight: Dissociation in psychosis is both prevalent and dangerous, associated with increased self-harm and suicidality, yet it is routinely missed in clinical assessment. This paper fills a critical training gap by providing a clear, actionable framework that does not require therapists to learn entirely new models — instead, it maps dissociation onto familiar CBT techniques. For private practitioners who may encounter psychotic-spectrum clients or complex trauma presentations with dissociative features, this guidance offers a structured approach to a phenomenon that often leaves clinicians feeling unskilled. The 'felt sense of anomaly' formulation is particularly useful for distinguishing dissociation from psychotic symptoms that require different management.
relevant_for: CBT therapists and clinical psychologists working with first-episode psychosis or trauma-related dissociation in adult populations

title: Generative AI-Enabled Therapy Support Tool Improves Clinical Outcomes and Patient Engagement in NHS Talking Therapies
summary: This real-world observational study (N=137) tested whether a generative AI-powered personalised therapy support tool deployed between CBT sessions could improve treatment outcomes in UK NHS Talking Therapies services. Patients using the AI tool (n=93) showed greater session attendance, fewer dropouts, and higher rates of reliable improvement, recovery, and reliable recovery compared to standard CBT worksheet controls (n=44). Within the intervention group, greater usage of the AI tool predicted more sessions attended and fewer dropouts. The study suggests that AI-enabled between-session support can meaningfully enhance CBT engagement and outcomes.
clinical_insight: Between-session homework non-compliance is one of the most persistent barriers to successful CBT. This study provides early but compelling evidence that AI-powered personalisation of between-session materials can substantially improve engagement and clinical outcomes. For private practice clinicians, this signals a near-future where AI tools could handle the 'homework gap' — personalising psychoeducation, behavioural experiments, and thought records to each client's specific presentation and progress. A critical caveat is the observational design: without randomisation, the improvement may partly reflect patient motivation rather than tool efficacy. The ethical implications of AI in therapeutic spaces also warrant careful consideration, particularly around data privacy and the therapeutic relationship.
relevant_for: CBT practitioners and clinic directors interested in technology-enhanced therapy delivery for depression and anxiety

title: Underground Small-Group Therapy of Treatment-Resistant Depression and C-PTSD with Psilocybin: A Retrospective Case Study
summary: This retrospective case study details the complete treatment process of a woman in her early forties with three decades of treatment-resistant depression rooted in early complex trauma (domestic violence, sexual abuse, poverty). After antidepressant medications proved ineffective and caused adverse effects, she participated in approximately six psilocybin sessions over 1.5 years within an underground small-group format combining breathing exercises, cold exposure, physical exercise, and music. Psilocybin appeared to produce an 'anti-dissociative' effect, interrupting trauma-related dissociation and allowing controlled re-experiencing of previously overwhelming somatic sensations. Depression remission was achieved and maintained at 2.5-year follow-up. The author argues that current clinical trials using only one or two doses may be insufficient for complex trauma presentations.
clinical_insight: While this is a single-case retrospective study from an uncontrolled setting — limiting generalisability — it offers a detailed clinical narrative that resonates with therapists working treatment-resistant complex trauma. The concept of psilocybin as an 'anti-dissociative' that reconnects patients with embodied traumatic memory is clinically provocative and aligns with emerging theories of psychedelic-assisted therapy. The key clinical insight is the multi-dose, extended-timeline argument: current trial designs may underestimate the number of sessions needed for C-PTSD remission. Clinicians should be aware that patients with complex trauma histories who pursue psychedelic therapy may need more than the standard one-to-two session protocol to achieve lasting change.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists and clinicians whose patients are considering or undergoing psychedelic-assisted therapy for treatment-resistant depression and C-PTSD

title: MDMA, Internal Family Systems Therapy, and the Minnesota Model in the Resolution of C-PTSD-Induced Alcohol and Diazepam Addiction: A Retrospective Case Study
summary: This case study illustrates the integrated use of MDMA-assisted therapy combined with Internal Family Systems (IFS) for a man in his forties with C-PTSD-induced addiction to alcohol and diazepam, rooted in witnessing severe chronic domestic violence in childhood. After multiple failed quit attempts and a suicidal crisis, the patient completed a 28-day Minnesota-model abstinence retreat, achieving initial but fragile sobriety. A single MDMA-assisted IFS session then allowed him to safely re-experience previously dissociated traumatic memories in an embodied way, resolving core C-PTSD symptoms. A subsequent non-drug IFS session consolidated gains. The case demonstrates how MDMA may facilitate the IFS process of accessing and compassionately engaging with exile parts carrying traumatic burden.
clinical_insight: This is the first recent case study to detail the practical integration of MDMA with IFS — a combination with strong theoretical alignment, as both approaches emphasise compassionate engagement with protective and wounded parts of the psyche. For clinicians in Australia (where MDMA is now prescribable for PTSD) and those preparing for potential regulatory changes elsewhere, this case provides a concrete clinical roadmap. The sequence matters: abstinence stabilisation first, then MDMA-assisted processing of the deepest traumatic material, followed by non-drug IFS consolidation. The case highlights that substance addiction in C-PTSD often functions as avoidance of emotional pain, and that resolving the underlying trauma rather than targeting the addiction directly may produce more durable outcomes. The single-case design and underground treatment setting limit evidential strength.
relevant_for: Addiction and trauma therapists, particularly those integrating IFS or preparing for MDMA-assisted therapy practice

title: Emotion Dysregulation in Nonsuicidal Self-Injury: Dissociations Between Global Self-Reports and Real-Time Responses to Emotional Challenge
summary: This study compared young adults with past-year NSSI (n=51) and controls (n=50) using both real-time (heart rate, HRV, electrodermal activity during stress induction) and retrospective self-report measures of emotional responding. Despite the NSSI group reporting significantly greater emotion dysregulation on global questionnaires, both groups showed remarkably similar subjective and physiological reactivity and recovery during the laboratory emotional challenge. Both groups also used similar cognitive reappraisal and expressive suppression strategies, and remembered the emotional challenge similarly at two-week follow-up. These findings challenge dominant theories that NSSI is driven by amplified emotional responses.
clinical_insight: This study delivers a significant challenge to the widely held assumption that NSSI is characterised by heightened emotional reactivity. The dissociation between self-reported and real-time emotional responding suggests that people who self-injure may perceive their emotion regulation as poorer than their actual in-the-moment capacity — or that global self-report measures capture a different construct than laboratory challenges. For therapists, this has immediate implications: assessment should not rely solely on trait-level questionnaires, and interventions that target presumed emotional hypersensitivity may be misdirected. Instead, the clinical focus might better address the beliefs and narratives clients hold about their emotional lives, the meaning they ascribe to emotional experiences, and the contextual triggers that lead to self-injury despite adequate real-time regulation capacity. A limitation is the mild and sporadic NSSI in the sample, which may not generalise to clinical populations with severe, frequent self-injury.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating nonsuicidal self-injury in adult mental health settings

title: Self-Harm in Eating Disorders: Identifying Contextual Patterns Using an Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
summary: This EMA study recruited 43 participants from specialist eating disorder outpatient services, prompting them six times daily for 14 days via smartphone app. Seventy-eight percent reported at least one self-harm thought and 42% reported at least one self-harm act. When self-harm thoughts arose, participants were commonly alone, experiencing worry, and considering cutting. Actual self-harm acts were preceded by 1-30 minutes of self-harm rumination and were accompanied by self-hatred. The findings highlight the co-morbidity of eating disorders and self-harm and the need for co-designed safety planning in outpatient ED services.
clinical_insight: This study provides the first granular, momentary-level map of when and how self-harm unfolds in eating disorder patients in their daily lives. For clinicians working with ED populations, the key takeaway is that self-harm thoughts often emerge in the context of worry and solitude, with a build-up period of 1-30 minutes before acting — a window that safety plans can explicitly target. The high co-occurrence of self-harm thoughts (78%) in a specialist outpatient sample underscores that ED clinicians should routinely assess for self-harm rather than treating it as a secondary concern. A notable blind spot is the exclusion of participants under 16, which may underestimate self-harm prevalence in younger ED populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians in eating disorder outpatient services who routinely assess and manage self-harm risk

title: Affective Suppression Fatigue: A Neurocognitive Framework for Emotional Numbing and Reactive Dysregulation
summary: This paper introduces Affective Suppression Fatigue (ASF), a neurocognitive model proposing that chronic expressive suppression degrades the brain regulatory systems themselves, particularly prefrontal inhibitory control. The model explains two paradoxical outcomes: emotional numbing (affective blunting under low-intensity conditions) and reactive dysregulation (explosive emotional outbursts under high-intensity conditions). By integrating findings on cognitive depletion, burnout, and chronic stress, ASF offers a unified framework for understanding why long-term emotion suppression often backfires, producing the very dysregulation it was intended to prevent.
clinical_insight: Many patients in private practice present with a pattern of emotional constriction punctuated by sudden dysregulation — a clinical presentation that existing models (gross emotion regulation, dialectical behaviour theory) explain only partially. The ASF model gives clinicians a coherent explanatory framework for this common pattern, linking the patient chronic suppression habit to observable neurocognitive degradation. This is particularly useful for psychoeducation with patients who intellectualise emotions or present with alexithymia — explaining that suppression itself damages the regulatory system may motivate engagement with experiential approaches. The framework also bridges CBT, ACT, and psychodynamic perspectives by identifying a shared mechanism. A limitation is that the model is theoretical; empirical validation is needed.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with patients who present with emotional numbing, alexithymia, or reactive dysregulation patterns across mood, trauma, and personality disorder presentations

title: Network Analysis on the Efficacy of the Unified Protocol for Transdiagnostic Cognitive Behavioral Therapy of Emotional Disorders
summary: Using multilevel vector autoregressive (ML-VAR) network models, this study analysed time-series data from 123 adult patients (M=37.9 years) with emotional disorders undergoing the Unified Protocol (UP) for transdiagnostic CBT in Japanese trials. Functional impairment nodes (both depressive and anxiety-related) emerged as the most central in the temporal network structure. Non-responders had significantly denser network structures than responders, suggesting that treatment resistance may be characterised by more tightly interconnected symptom-impairment cycles. Between-treatment and end-of-treatment cross-sectional network comparisons showed no significant structural changes, challenging the assumption that treatment simply reduces overall connectivity.
clinical_insight: This study offers the first process-level network analysis of the Unified Protocol in action, and its findings have direct clinical implications. The centrality of functional impairment nodes suggests that clinicians using the UP should monitor functional recovery alongside symptom reduction — impairment may be the engine that maintains the symptom network rather than a mere consequence. The finding that non-responders show denser networks points to a potential early-warning marker: patients whose symptom-impairment connections remain highly interconnected early in treatment may need a modified approach, such as more intensive exposure work or adjunctive interventions. The tension between the null cross-sectional results and the meaningful temporal findings highlights a debate in network psychopathology about whether treatment changes network structure or simply weakens individual node activation. The adult sample (mean age 38) makes findings directly relevant to private practice.
relevant_for: CBT clinicians using the Unified Protocol or transdiagnostic approaches for adult anxiety and depression

title: Obsessive-Compulsive Symptoms and Sound Sensitivities: Comparing Misophonia and Hyperacusis
summary: This multi-trait, multi-method study examined the relationship between obsessive-compulsive symptoms and two types of sound sensitivity — misophonia and hyperacusis — in a large online sample (N=1,094) with a subset completing in-person audiological and psychological assessments (N=102). Results showed that O-C symptoms were associated with both misophonia and hyperacusis, and that this association was consistent regardless of measurement method (questionnaire, clinician rating, or laboratory assessment). Depression was less strongly associated with sound sensitivities than O-C symptoms, suggesting the link is not an artifact of general distress. The findings indicate that sensory sensitivities broadly, rather than misophonia specifically, are linked to O-C symptoms.
clinical_insight: Patients with OCD frequently report sound sensitivities, yet the nature of this relationship has been unclear. This study clarifies that the O-C/sound sensitivity link is robust across measurement methods and extends beyond misophonia to hyperacusis. For clinicians, this means that assessing for sound sensitivities in OCD patients is warranted as part of a comprehensive formulation — these sensory issues may maintain compulsive behaviours (e.g., avoidance of triggering environments, mental rituals to neutralise distress). The finding that general distress does not fully account for the association pushes back against the tendency to dismiss sensory complaints as non-specific anxiety symptoms. A limitation is the university student sample, which may not represent clinical OCD populations with greater symptom severity.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating OCD who encounter comorbid sound sensitivity complaints

title: The Most Vulnerable Are Prone to Use AI Therapists: The Role of Attachment, Epistemic Trust, and Mental Health Symptoms in Acceptance of Digital Mental Health Interventions
summary: Surveying 1,612 potential patients and clinicians, this study used cluster analysis to identify three psychological profiles: Avoidant-not trusting, Secure-trusting-healthy, and Young-anxious-gullible-symptomatic. Paradoxically, the two most psychologically vulnerable clusters showed significantly higher acceptance of AI-based digital mental health interventions (avatars, chatbots), while the healthiest cluster preferred human-delivered teletherapy. Prior therapy experience was associated with lower AI acceptance overall. The authors suggest AI-based tools may serve as a critical entry point to care for individuals who struggle with human-to-human therapeutic relationships, but caution that these same individuals may miss out on the relational learning inherent in human therapy.
clinical_insight: As AI therapy tools proliferate, this study provides essential nuance for private practitioners. The paradoxical finding — that the most vulnerable patients are most drawn to AI therapists — carries significant clinical implications. On one hand, AI tools may lower the threshold for engaging patients who would otherwise avoid therapy due to attachment-related fears or epistemic mistrust. On the other, precisely these patients have the most to gain from the corrective relational experience that only a human therapist can provide. Clinicians should be prepared to discuss AI therapy options with avoidant or anxious patients not as a replacement for therapy, but as a potential stepping stone. A blind spot is the Prolific recruitment method, which may not capture the most treatment-averse individuals who never engage with any mental health platform.
relevant_for: Private practice clinicians considering how to position their services relative to emerging AI therapy tools, and those working with attachment-disrupted or treatment-reluctant patients

title: Self-Treatment of Depression and Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder with Psilocybin and LSD: A Retrospective Case Study
summary: This retrospective case study describes a young man with early-onset complex trauma from chronic school-based abuse who developed severe, treatment-resistant anxiety and depression. After supportive psychotherapy failed and psychiatric contact was experienced as rejecting, he turned to unsupervised self-treatment with psilocybin at age 19. Occasional high-dose sessions alone produced only temporary relief. From age 25, he adopted a more intensive, structured approach with gradually declining doses, focusing on reliving traumatic events while maintaining conscious awareness of somatic sensations and avoiding overwhelm.
