By age 30, he reported resolving most early trauma and achieving a foundational sense of stability, though societal trauma exposure continued to cause suffering. The author proposes reconceptualising psychedelics as tools for understanding ordinary mental states rather than pathological ones.
clinical_insight: This case is notable for its detailed documentation of a self-directed, multi-year psychedelic process — a reality many clinicians will encounter as patients increasingly present with unsupervised psychedelic use histories. The key clinical insight is the patient own discovery that gradual dose reduction combined with sustained somatic focus produced better results than occasional high-dose sessions, directly challenging the prevailing clinical trial model of single or double high-dose administration. The case also illustrates how iatrogenic harm from psychiatric services (experienced rejection) can push patients toward self-treatment, raising questions about how clinicians receive and respond to patients psychedelic experiences. A significant limitation is the retrospective, self-reported nature of the data, and the absence of standardised outcome measures.
relevant_for: Trauma therapists whose patients have used or are considering using psychedelics for self-treatment of C-PTSD and treatment-resistant depression

title: Qualitative Analysis of a Patient Experience of Ketamine-Assisted Psychotherapy for Substance Use Disorder: Empirical Synergies with Twelve-Step Programs
summary: This case report details a 28-year-old woman treated for alcohol and prescription opioid use disorder using an inter-clinic collaborative treatment plan combining ketamine-assisted psychotherapy (KAP) with existing 12-step-based psychosocial treatments. Qualitative analysis of the patient lived experience revealed that ketamine-induced experiences meaningfully overlapped with 12-step concepts such as powerlessness, spiritual awakening, and moral inventory. The authors detail specific clinical practices for managing the unique risks of using ketamine — itself a substance with recreational potential — as a treatment for SUD, and highlight the synergies between psychedelic experiences and 12-step recovery frameworks.
clinical_insight: As ketamine clinics expand rapidly, the question of how to integrate KAP with established addiction treatment frameworks like 12-step programs is increasingly urgent. This case report provides a practical clinical roadmap: rather than positioning KAP as an alternative to 12-step work, the authors demonstrate how ketamine experiences can deepen engagement with recovery concepts such as surrender, spiritual experience, and self-examination. For addiction clinicians, the detailed risk-management practices for using a psychoactive substance to treat substance use disorder are particularly valuable. The case highlights a tension in the field: patients may attribute recovery to the ketamine experience itself, while clinicians may see it as a catalyst for engagement with existing therapeutic frameworks. A limitation is the single-case design and the specific cultural context of 12-step programs, which may not generalise to all treatment approaches.
relevant_for: Addiction clinicians integrating ketamine-assisted psychotherapy with 12-step or other structured psychosocial treatment programs

title: MDMA-Assisted Therapy for Complex-Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: Toward a Neurocognitive Account
summary: This paper proposes a novel neurocognitive model explaining how MDMA-assisted therapy may specifically benefit individuals with complex PTSD (C-PTSD). Drawing on active inference and self-modelling theories, the authors elucidate the insula’s role in affective regulation and how its dysfunction underpins C-PTSD symptoms including affective dysregulation, negative self-concept, and interpersonal difficulties. The framework suggests MDMA may recalibrate affective regulation and reinforce a more stable self-model, distinguishing its therapeutic potential for C-PTSD from standard PTSD approaches.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with C-PTSD patients should note the proposed distinction between standard PTSD (fear-conditioning based) and C-PTSD (self-model disruption). The insula-centric framework offers a rationale for why C-PTSD patients may benefit from MDMA’s prosocial and emotion-regulating effects beyond simple fear extinction. A notable blind spot: the model is purely theoretical and has not been empirically validated in C-PTSD populations specifically, so clinical application should await trial data.
relevant_for: Clinicians specializing in trauma therapy, particularly those working with patients who have prolonged interpersonal trauma histories and exhibit disturbances in self-organization

title: Exploring Psilocybin-Assisted Schema Therapy: A Conceptual Framework for Potential Therapeutic Synergies in Personality Disorders
summary: This conceptual paper proposes a novel integration of psilocybin-assisted therapy with Schema Therapy (PAST) for personality disorders. Within a predictive coding framework, the authors draw parallels between Early Maladaptive Schemas and high-level priors, arguing that psilocybin’s ability to relax entrenched beliefs (per the REBUS/REBAS models) may enhance Schema Therapy’s experiential and cognitive interventions. The model suggests combining psilocybin sessions with ST integration work to strengthen adaptive modes and reduce dysfunctional schema modes, potentially reducing treatment duration for Cluster B and C personality disorders.
clinical_insight: Schema therapists working with personality-disordered patients may find the predictive coding rationale useful for conceptualizing why some patients show rigid resistance to schema mode shifts. The proposed PAST model offers a theoretical justification for combining psychedelic-facilitated cognitive flexibility with established ST techniques. However, the authors appropriately caution that this remains conceptual—no clinical data yet support psilocybin use in PD populations, and the potential for destabilization in borderline patients requires careful consideration.
relevant_for: Schema therapists and clinicians working with personality disorders who are following developments in psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy

title: Day-to-Day Dynamics of Facial Emotion Expressions in Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
summary: This innovative study applied idiographic (person-specific) network modeling to daily facial emotion expressions and PTSD symptoms in 112 World Trade Center responders over 90 days, yielding 8,953 video diary recordings analyzed with facial emotion recognition. Eighty percent of participants showed at least one reliable expression-symptom link, with significant heterogeneity—some individuals’ symptoms related to emotional over-expressivity while others showed under-expressivity. The study moves beyond self-report to capture outward emotional behavior as a mechanism maintaining PTSD.
clinical_insight: The finding that PTSD emotion-symptom dynamics are highly individualized has direct implications for case conceptualization: clinicians cannot assume a uniform emotional presentation in PTSD. Some patients may benefit from interventions targeting emotional expression (e.g., exposure-based approaches), while others may need help amplifying emotional awareness. The study’s strength lies in its ecological validity and idiographic approach, though the WTC responder sample limits generalizability to other trauma populations such as interpersonal trauma survivors.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating PTSD, particularly those interested in personalized case formulation and the role of emotional expression in symptom maintenance

title: Early Warning Signs of Bipolar Relapse: Investigating Critical Slowing Down in Smartphone Data
summary: This study examined whether smartphone-based digital phenotyping could detect early warning signals (increased autocorrelation and variance) before mood episodes in 29 bipolar disorder patients tracked over one year (10,587 patient-days). Activity-related measures predicted manic episodes, while sleep parameters predicted both manic and depressive transitions. However, even the best combined predictors did not meet predefined clinical utility thresholds on ROC analysis, indicating that while the dynamical systems approach shows promise, current predictive accuracy remains insufficient for clinical deployment.
clinical_insight: While digital phenotyping for bipolar disorder is not yet clinically actionable, this study demonstrates that passive smartphone data captures meaningful signal about impending episodes. Clinicians should be aware that patients increasingly use such tools and may bring self-tracked data into sessions. The honest reporting of below-threshold accuracy is methodologically commendable. The tension between research promise and clinical readiness remains unresolved—future work may benefit from combining digital phenotyping with clinician judgment rather than replacing it.
relevant_for: Clinicians managing bipolar disorder who are interested in digital mental health tools and relapse prevention strategies

title: Mental Imagery and Its Role in the Psychopathology and Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Systematic Review and Narrative Synthesis
summary: This systematic review examined the role of intrusive mental imagery in OCD psychopathology and treatment. Up to 89% of OCD patients reported experiencing intrusive imagery, often vivid and accompanied by bodily sensations, with 70-79% relating intrusions to adverse or traumatic events. Compared to other anxiety disorders, intrusive imagery in OCD occurred more frequently and often led patients to believe they possessed a ‘dangerous self.’ Imagery-based treatments including Imagery Rescripting and EMDR showed promise in reducing symptoms, offering an effective alternative for patients whose primary symptoms are imagery-based.
clinical_insight: This review provides strong justification for routinely assessing intrusive imagery in OCD patients, not just intrusive thoughts. The finding that most OCD imagery is trauma-linked suggests overlapping mechanisms with PTSD and supports trauma-informed approaches to OCD treatment. EMDR and Imagery Rescripting emerge as evidence-supported adjuncts for imagery-dominant OCD presentations. A blind spot: the review notes limited high-quality RCT data for imagery-based interventions in OCD, so while promising, these should be offered as complements rather than replacements for first-line treatments like ERP.
relevant_for: Clinicians treating OCD, especially those whose patients report vivid intrusive images or have comorbid trauma histories

title: Mapping Psychological (In)Flexibility in U.S. Military Veterans: A Directed Acyclic Graph Approach to the Hexaflex Model
summary: Using Directed Acyclic Graphs in a sample of 283 U.S. military veterans, this study mapped potential causal pathways between components of the ACT Hexaflex model. Committed action and acceptance emerged as central drivers of psychological flexibility, while cognitive fusion appeared to initiate cascades of inflexibility—flowing through self-as-content and inaction to experiential avoidance, diminished present-moment awareness, and lost values contact. The study provides the first DAG-based analysis of the Hexaflex in a veteran population at elevated risk for mental health complaints.
clinical_insight: The identification of cognitive fusion as the primary entry point into inflexibility cascades has clear clinical implications: targeting defusion early in ACT treatment for veterans may disrupt downstream dysfunction across multiple Hexaflex processes. The central role of committed action in flexibility pathways supports behavioral activation as a key mechanism of change. Clinicians should note that these are cross-sectional pathways, not confirmed causal mechanisms, though the DAG approach provides stronger causal inference than standard correlational analyses.
relevant_for: ACT practitioners working with veterans or trauma-exposed populations, particularly those interested in process-based therapy and mechanisms of change

title: Resilience in Sexual Assault Victimization: Do Mindfulness and Emotion Regulation Buffer Associations with Post-Traumatic Stress?
summary: This study compared post-traumatic stress severity across three victimization profiles in 247 cisgender women aged 21-30: childhood sexual abuse only, adulthood sexual assault only, and revictimized (both CSA and ASA). Revictimized participants showed the highest PTSS severity, followed by ASA-only and CSA-only. Mindfulness and emotion regulation were directly associated with reduced PTSS across all profiles, suggesting these psychological skills buffer trauma symptoms regardless of victimization timing or pattern.
clinical_insight: The finding that mindfulness and emotion regulation reduce PTSS across all victimization profiles—including the most severe revictimization group—supports incorporating these skills into trauma-focused treatments. For therapists, this reinforces the value of teaching mindfulness-based and emotion regulation strategies (e.g., DBT skills, ACT mindfulness) as transdiagnostic protective factors rather than as primary treatments. A limitation: the cross-sectional design cannot establish causation, and the sample was restricted to cisgender women aged 21-30, limiting generalizability to male survivors, older adults, or gender-diverse individuals.
relevant_for: Clinicians working with sexual trauma survivors, particularly those treating young adult women with complex victimization histories

title: Machine Learning Prediction of Treatment Response to Internet-Delivered Cognitive Behavioural Therapy vs. Antidepressant Medication
summary: This study developed and validated a multivariable predictive model for iCBT treatment response using real-world data from 776 patients. An elastic net regression with 27 predictors (including baseline depression severity, treatment expectation, and transdiagnostic symptoms) explained 14.6% of variance in depression change. A key finding was that models trained exclusively on iCBT patients showed treatment-specificity (R2=19.3% for iCBT vs. 10.8% for antidepressant-only), suggesting that treatment-specific models may better guide personalized treatment selection.
clinical_insight: This study supports the feasibility of using routine self-report data to guide treatment selection between iCBT and pharmacotherapy for depression. Clinicians should note that treatment expectation emerged as a key predictor, reinforcing the importance of expectancy management early in therapy. The finding that treatment-specific models outperformed mixed-treatment models has a notable blind spot: 24-34% of participants were concurrently receiving both treatments, potentially inflating cross-treatment generalizability. Nonetheless, this represents a concrete step toward personalized treatment matching in routine care.
relevant_for: Clinicians involved in treatment selection for depression, particularly those offering or referring to internet-based CBT programs

title: Cost-Utility Analysis of Internet-Delivered Cognitive Control Training Plus Treatment as Usual Versus Treatment as Usual Alone for Relapse Prevention in Major Depressive Disorder
summary: This health economic evaluation assessed the cost-utility of adding internet-delivered Cognitive Control Training (aPASAT) to treatment as usual for adults in remission from MDD. Using a Markov model over a five-year time horizon from the Belgian healthcare payer perspective, aPASAT + TAU dominated TAU alone, yielding additional QALYs and lower healthcare costs. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis indicated a 99.9% probability of cost-effectiveness at a €48,000/QALY threshold, with scenario analyses consistently confirming cost savings and improved outcomes.
clinical_insight: The finding that a fully unguided, low-cost digital intervention can both improve outcomes and reduce costs has significant implications for stepped-care models in depression relapse prevention. Clinicians working in primary care or mental health systems should consider recommending CCT as a scalable prevention tool for remitted MDD patients. A notable blind spot: the model parameters rely heavily on published literature rather than primary trial data for the economic evaluation, so real-world implementation may yield different cost profiles. The tension between the promise of scalable digital prevention and the need for long-term adherence data remains unresolved.
relevant_for: Clinicians and healthcare administrators designing relapse prevention pathways for major depressive disorder

title: Internet Cognitive Therapy for Prolonged Grief Disorder (iCT-PG): A Developmental Case Series
summary: This study evaluated a novel 12-session, therapist-assisted internet cognitive therapy adapted from iCT-PTSD for Prolonged Grief Disorder (iCT-PG). Eight adults meeting PGD criteria completed the program over 14 weeks, targeting loss-related memory characteristics, negative grief appraisals, social disconnection, and unhelpful coping. All participants completed treatment, with 87.5% showing both reliable improvement and recovery below clinical cut-offs. Large reductions were observed in comorbid PTSD, depression, anxiety, and functional impairment, maintained at 3-month follow-up. Average therapist time was approximately 10 hours per participant.
clinical_insight: The adaptation of evidence-based CT for PTSD to target PGD-specific maintenance mechanisms represents a promising approach for a disorder with limited treatment access. The strong recovery rate (87.5%) and low therapist time (10 hours) support the scalability of this format. Clinicians should note that the intervention explicitly targeted cognitive mechanisms (negative grief appraisals, memory characteristics) rather than solely providing supportive counseling. The primary blind spot is the uncontrolled design and tiny sample—these results cannot distinguish treatment effects from natural remission or therapist attention, though the consistency of improvement across multiple outcome domains is encouraging.
relevant_for: Grief therapists and clinicians working with prolonged grief disorder, particularly those seeking scalable digital treatment options

title: When “Bothered by” Means “Often”: Evidence for Misinterpretation of the Patient Health Questionnaire
summary: This study investigated how respondents interpret the PHQ’s instructions across a general population sample (N=503) and a clinical sample with elevated depression (N=349). Only 54.7% of community participants and 15.5% of the clinical sample interpreted the PHQ as instructed (frequency of being bothered). When asked directly, only 21.3% and 11.7% respectively had responded based on the instructed interpretation, with even fewer intending to do so in the future. PHQ-8 validity varied depending on how participants interpreted the instructions, raising questions about whether the measure captures symptom frequency, distress severity, or some combination.
clinical_insight: This study has immediate clinical implications: if only 11-21% of respondents interpret the PHQ as intended, then depression severity scores in both research and clinical practice are systematically contaminated by response interpretation variance. Clinicians should be aware that a patient reporting being “bothered a lot” may be reporting frequency rather than distress, or vice versa. The finding that clinical participants were even less likely to interpret instructions correctly than community participants suggests that severity of symptoms may further distort comprehension. A blind spot: the study cannot determine which interpretation produces more valid depression measurement—it only demonstrates that the current instrument conflates distinct constructs.
relevant_for: All clinicians using the PHQ-9, PHQ-8, or PHQ-2 for depression screening or outcome monitoring

title: Diminished Positive Affect in Social Anhedonia: Linking Anticipation and Experience During Dyadic Interaction
summary: This preregistered study examined how social anhedonia relates to affective responses before, during, and after dyadic social interactions in 100 young adults. Controlling for social anxiety and suspiciousness, social anhedonia was specifically associated with diminished positive affect both in anticipation of and during social interaction, but not with increased negative affect. Negative affect during interaction was specifically explained by co-occurring social anxiety. An indirect effect of social anhedonia via diminished positive experience on social motivation and volition was also found.
clinical_insight: The dissociation between social anhedonia (diminished positive affect) and social anxiety (increased negative affect) has direct implications for differential diagnosis and treatment planning. Clinicians often encounter patients presenting with social withdrawal who may have either or both conditions. This study suggests that treatments should be tailored: social anhedonia may respond to behavioral activation targeting reward processing and positive affect forecasting, whereas social anxiety requires exposure-based approaches. The exploratory finding that socially anhedonic participants had a comforting effect on their interaction partners warrants investigation. A limitation: the WEIRD young adult sample limits generalizability to older clinical populations.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing and treating adults with social withdrawal, social anhedonia, or social anxiety disorder

title: Anxiety Identity Centrality Is Associated With Avoidant Coping in Anxious Adults
summary: In a large sample of 1,234 high-anxiety adults, this study examined whether anxiety identity centrality—seeing one’s anxiety as important to one’s self-concept—predicts avoidant coping. Using Bayesian ordered probit regression, anxiety identity centrality showed small-to-medium correlations with situational (r=.42) and experiential (r=.12) avoidance. Crucially, these associations held after controlling for anxiety symptom severity, suggesting that identity centrality contributes to avoidance beyond symptom level alone. The construct was measurable with a single-item ordinal measure.
clinical_insight: This study introduces a clinically relevant construct with direct therapeutic implications: patients who have incorporated anxiety into their identity may resist treatment approaches that could reduce their anxiety, as improvement would threaten their self-concept. Clinicians should consider assessing anxiety identity centrality early in treatment, particularly for patients who seem stuck in avoidant patterns despite adequate symptom-focused intervention. The debate about whether labeling mental health conditions promotes or hinders recovery is given empirical grounding here. A blind spot: the cross-sectional design cannot establish whether anxiety centrality causes avoidance or emerges from chronic anxiety experiences, limiting causal inferences for treatment planning.
relevant_for: CBT and ACT practitioners working with anxious adults who show chronic avoidant coping patterns

title: When Happiness Becomes Aversive: A Meta-Analysis of Fear of Happiness in Depression and Anxiety
summary: This meta-analysis examined associations between fear of happiness—a construct representing reward devaluation—and psychopathology in depression and anxiety. The analysis synthesized available evidence on how aversive responses to positive affect relate to symptom severity and treatment outcomes. The construct is particularly relevant because many current interventions target reward deficits in depression and anxiety, yet fear of happiness may undermine the efficacy of these reward-based approaches by making positive emotional experiences feel threatening or unsafe.
clinical_insight: Fear of happiness represents an underrecognized mechanism that may explain why some patients with depression or anxiety fail to benefit from interventions targeting positive affect or reward processing (e.g., behavioral activation, positive psychology interventions). Clinicians should be alert to patients who resist, avoid, or feel distressed by positive experiences, as this may indicate fear of happiness rather than simple anhedonia. This construct bridges the literatures on reward processing, emotion regulation, and clinical intervention. A key blind spot: the meta-analysis highlights the current lack of validated, brief clinical measures for fear of happiness, limiting its integration into routine assessment. The tension between treating reward deficits and inadvertently triggering reward devaluation remains a challenge for intervention design.
relevant_for: Clinicians using behavioral activation, positive psychology, or well-being interventions who encounter patients who resist positive experiences

title: Enhancing the Efficacy of Exposure Therapy: Translation of Pharmacological Augmentation of Fear Extinction
summary: This critical review examines translational research on pharmacological enhancement of fear extinction, focusing on D-cycloserine (DCS)—the most extensively studied agent. Despite early promise, results across preclinical, human laboratory, and clinical trials have been mixed. The authors identify factors accounting for inconsistent findings including study design differences, subject selection, sample size, and measurement approaches. They argue for a rigorous mechanistic research agenda that distinguishes between acquisition, consolidation, and retrieval of extinction processes as separate targets, and examines the relationship between changes in these processes and clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: The mixed evidence on DCS augmentation of exposure therapy has left many clinicians uncertain about its utility. This review provides a framework for understanding why results have been inconsistent and offers a roadmap for future research. For practicing clinicians, the key takeaway is that pharmacological augmentation of exposure remains experimental rather than ready for routine clinical use. The debate over whether DCS enhances extinction learning, consolidation, or retrieval has direct implications for when and how it should be administered relative to exposure sessions. A significant blind spot: the review largely focuses on DCS, leaving other potentially promising augmentation agents (e.g., MDMA, psilocybin) outside its scope.
relevant_for: Exposure therapists and researchers interested in pharmacological augmentation strategies for anxiety disorder treatment
